ai_analysis.human_story: Type `dd if=/dev/zero of=test bs=1M count=1024`. The 'M' here is from 1990s extensions, but the lowercase 'k=1024' you'd write as `bs=1k` is from 1979's dd. The pattern вҖ” number followed by unit suffix вҖ” spread to du -h, ls -lh, sleep '5m'. The seed: dd's number() with 5 cases. The `*`/`x` recursion enables nested multiplication: `2*3*4` = 24. K&R intentional fall-through routes the result through one range check. Compact, recursive, expressive вҖ” all in 35 lines.

appraiser_note: dd гҒ® number() й–ўж•°гҒҜ **еҚҳдҪҚгӮөгғ•гӮЈгғғгӮҜгӮ№д»ҳгҒҚж•ҙж•°**гӮ’гғ‘гғјгӮ№вҖ”вҖ”`k` гҒ§ Г— 1024 (kilo)гҖҒ `w` гҒ§ Г— sizeof(int) (word: PDP-11 гҒ§ 2гҖҒ VAX гҒ§ 4 гҒ®гғ—гғ©гғғгғҲгғ•гӮ©гғјгғ дҫқеӯҳеҖӨ)гҖҒ `b` гҒ§ Г— 512 (block: V7 BSIZE)гҖҒ `*`/`x` гҒ§ж¬ЎгҒ®ж•°еҖӨгҒЁд№—з®— (еҶҚеё°е‘јгҒіеҮәгҒ—)гҖӮ `2*1024`гҖҒ `100k`гҖҒ `4w`гҖҒ `2k*4` гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰеӢ•гҒҸгҖӮ case '*'/'x' гҒҜ case '\0' гҒ« **fall-through** гҒ—гҒҰзҜ„еӣІгғҒгӮ§гғғгӮҜгӮ’йҖҡгӮӢвҖ”вҖ”K&R жөҒгҒ®ж„Ҹеӣізҡ„гғ•гӮ©гғјгғ«гӮ№гғ«гғјгҖӮ еҫҢгҒ® du -hгҖҒ ls -lhгҖҒ sleep '5m' гҒ®ж•°еҖӨ+еҚҳдҪҚ UX гҒ®зҘ–гҖӮ GNU coreutils гҒҢ K/M/G/T (еӨ§ж–Үеӯ— SI жҺҘй ӯиҫһ) гӮ’ 1990 е№ҙд»ЈгҒ«иҝҪеҠ гҒ—гҒҹгҒҢгҖҒ POSIX dd гҒҜе°Ҹж–Үеӯ— k=1024 гӮ’з¶ӯжҢҒгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: jan1(yr) computes the day of week of January 1 of given year in 6 lines. Base formula: d = 4 + y + (y+3)/4 вҖ” offset 4 (year 0 alignment), +y (each year shifts day-of-week by 1 for 365-day non-leap years), +(y+3)/4 (Julian leap rule: every 4 years adds extra day, +3 offset for year 1 alignment). For y > 1800: subtract (y-1701)/100 (Gregorian 100-year non-leap correction starting 1801), add (y-1601)/400 (Gregorian 400-year leap correction starting 1601). For y > 1752: add 3 (compensate for British Calendar Act 1750 changeover). Final d%7: 0=Sun, 1=Mon, ..., 6=Sat. Verified: 2026 вҶ’ d = 2538 % 7 = 4 (Thursday).
ai_analysis.human_story: Six lines of arithmetic compute any year's January 1 day-of-week, from year 1 to 9999. The trick: Julian calendar contributions are linear (year + leap), Gregorian corrections are conditional (post-1800, post-1752). The +3 at the 1752 boundary embeds British history into the formula. Zeller published a similar formula in 1887; this 1979 K&R version is more compact, hiding centuries of calendar reform inside `if (y > 1752) d += 3`.

appraiser_note: jan1(yr) гҒҜ **6 иЎҢ**гҒ§д»»ж„Ҹе№ҙгҒ® Jan 1 гҒ®жӣңж—ҘгӮ’иЁҲз®—гҖӮ еҹәжң¬ејҸ `d = 4+y+(y+3)/4`вҖ”вҖ”гӮӘгғ•гӮ»гғғгғҲ 4 (иҘҝжҡҰе…ғе№ҙиө·зӮ№)гҖҒ +y (еҗ„е№ҙгҒ§жӣңж—Ҙ 1 гҒҡгӮҢ)гҖҒ +(y+3)/4 (гғҰгғӘгӮҰгӮ№й–Ҹе№ҙиҰҸеүҮ)гҖӮ `y > 1800` гҒ®гҒЁгҒҚ: `-= (y-1701)/100` (гӮ°гғ¬гӮҙгғӘгӮӘ 100 е№ҙйқһй–Ҹе№ҙиЈңжӯЈгҖҒ 1801 гҒӢгӮү)гҖҒ `+= (y-1601)/400` (гӮ°гғ¬гӮҙгғӘгӮӘ 400 е№ҙй–Ҹе№ҙиЈңжӯЈгҖҒ 1601 гҒӢгӮү)гҖӮ `y > 1752` гҒ®гҒЁгҒҚ: `+= 3` (гӮӨгӮ®гғӘгӮ№ Calendar Act 1750 иЈңжӯЈ)гҖӮ жңҖеҫҢгҒ« `% 7` гҒ§жӣңж—Ҙ (0=ж—Ҙ, 1=жңҲ, ..., 6=еңҹ)гҖӮ жӨңиЁјпјҡ2026 е№ҙ вҶ’ d = 2538 % 7 = 4 (жңЁжӣң) жӯЈи§ЈгҖӮ Zeller гҒ®еҗҲеҗҢејҸ (1887) гҒ®иҰӘжҲҡгҒ§гҖҒ гӮҲгӮҠз°ЎжҪ”гҖӮ `if (y > 1752) d += 3` гҒ® 1 иЎҢгҒ«ж•°дё–зҙҖгҒ®жҡҰж”№йқ©еҸІгҒҢеҹӢгӮҒиҫјгҒҫгӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: When cal sees year 1752, mon[9] becomes 19 instead of 30. The for-loop iterating from i=1 to mon[m] detects `i==3 && mon[m]==19` and jumps: i += 11 (next iteration is i=14), mon[m] += 11 (restore mon[9] to 30 so loop continues correctly). Output: `1 2 14 15 16 ...` вҖ” 11 days missing. This implements the British Calendar (New Style) Act 1750: September 2, 1752 (Wed, Julian) was followed by September 14, 1752 (Thu, Gregorian). 11 days dropped to align with the sun. Run `cal 9 1752` on any modern Linux/BSD/macOS вҖ” same gap, 50 years of Unix faithfully recording British calendar reform.
ai_analysis.human_story: Type `cal 9 1752` today on Linux, you see September 1752 with 11 days missing вҖ” 1, 2, then 14, 15, 16. Why? Britain adopted the Gregorian calendar in 1752. The 11-day drop aligned the British calendar with the sun (and with Continental Europe, which had switched in 1582). Workers reportedly rioted demanding their 11 days' wages back (some historians dispute this). 1979's cal.c carved this historical moment into 5 lines of C. Every Unix descendant carries it forward. The riot may be myth; the calendar is real, and so is the gap.

appraiser_note: 1752 е№ҙ 9 жңҲгҒ®гҒЁгҒҚ mon[9] = 19 гҒ«гҒ—гҖҒ for гғ«гғјгғ—гҒҢ `i==3 && mon[m]==19` гӮ’жӨңеҮәгҒҷгӮӢгҒЁ `i += 11` (ж¬ЎгҒ®гӮӨгғҶгғ¬гғјгӮ·гғ§гғігҒ§ i=14)гҖҒ `mon[m] += 11` (30 гҒ«жҲ»гҒҷ) гҒ§ 11 ж—ҘгӮ’йЈӣгҒ°гҒҷгҖӮ иЎЁзӨәгҒҜ `1 2 14 15 16 ...`вҖ”вҖ”11 ж—Ҙж¶ҲеӨұгҖӮ гӮӨгӮ®гғӘгӮ№ Calendar (New Style) Act 1750 гҒ®е®ҹиЈ…пјҡ1752 е№ҙ 9 жңҲ 2 ж—Ҙ (ж°ҙгҖҒ гғҰгғӘгӮҰгӮ№) гҒ®зҝҢж—ҘгҒҢ 9 жңҲ 14 ж—Ҙ (жңЁгҖҒ гӮ°гғ¬гӮҙгғӘгӮӘ)гҖӮ 11 ж—ҘгӮ’еүҠгҒЈгҒҰеӨӘйҷҪе№ҙгҒ«еҗҲгӮҸгҒӣгҒҹгҖӮ еӨ§йҷёгғЁгғјгғӯгғғгғ‘гҒҜ 1582 е№ҙгҒ«еҲҮжӣҝжёҲгҒҝ (10 ж—Ҙ)гҖҒ гӮӨгӮ®гғӘгӮ№зі»гҒҜ 1752 е№ҙ (11 ж—Ҙ)гҖҒ гғӯгӮ·гӮўгҒҜ 1918 е№ҙ (13 ж—Ҙ)гҖӮ 50 е№ҙеҫҢгҒ® Linux/BSD/macOS гҒ§ `cal 9 1752` гӮ’жү“гҒҰгҒ°еҗҢгҒҳ 11 ж—ҘгҒҢд»ҠгӮӮж¶ҲгҒҲгҒҹгҒҫгҒҫвҖ”вҖ”гӮӨгӮ®гғӘгӮ№гҒ®жҡҰж”№йқ©гҒҢ Unix зі»гҒ® DNA гҒ«еҲ»гҒҫгӮҢгҒҹзһ¬й–“гҖӮ еҠҙеғҚиҖ…гҒ®жҡҙеӢ• (11 ж—ҘеҲҶгҒ®иіғйҮ‘гӮ’иҝ”гҒӣ) гҒҜдҝ—иӘ¬гҒЁжӯҙеҸІе®¶гҒҜиЁҖгҒҶгҒҢгҖҒ жҡҰгҒҜжң¬зү©гҖҒ з©әзҷҪгӮӮжң¬зү©гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Instead of writing the conventional leap-year predicate `y%4==0 && (y%100!=0 || y%400==0)`, cal.c detects year-length via day-of-week shift: (jan1(y+1) + 7 - d) % 7 where d = jan1(y). Non-leap year (365 days = 52 weeks + 1 day) вҶ’ shift = 1. Leap year (366 days = 52 weeks + 2 days) вҶ’ shift = 2. Year 1752 (354 days = 365 - 11) вҶ’ shift = 4 (default case). The +7 prevents negative subtraction when jan1(y+1) < d (e.g. Mon вҶ’ Sun wraps). One switch handles all three cases (regular, leap, 1752) using a single jan1() function that already encodes the Gregorian/Julian transition.
ai_analysis.human_story: Most code asks 'is this a leap year?' with explicit divisibility. cal.c asks 'how many days in this year?' via day-of-week diff. The clever answer: 1, 2, or 4. The same switch handles regular, leap, and the once-in-history 1752 short year. By centralizing calendar logic in jan1() (the Julian/Gregorian transitions live there), the leap detection inherits those corrections for free. Three case branches replace what would otherwise be 10+ lines of date-arithmetic special-casing.

appraiser_note: йҖҡеёёгҒ®й–Ҹе№ҙеҲӨе®ҡ `y%4==0 && (y%100!=0 || y%400==0)` гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸгҖҒ **жӣңж—Ҙе·®**гҒ§ 1 е№ҙгҒ®ж—Ҙж•°гӮ’йҖҶз®—гҒҷгӮӢзӢ¬еүөзҡ„гӮўгғ—гғӯгғјгғҒпјҡ`(jan1(y+1) + 7 - d) % 7`гҖӮ е№іе№ҙ 365 ж—Ҙ = 52 йҖұ + 1 ж—Ҙ вҶ’ shift = 1гҖҒ й–Ҹе№ҙ 366 ж—Ҙ = 52 йҖұ + 2 ж—Ҙ вҶ’ shift = 2гҖҒ 1752 е№ҙ 354 ж—Ҙ = 365 - 11 вҶ’ shift = 4 (default)гҖӮ `+7` гҒҜ `jan1(y+1) < d` гҒ®гҒЁгҒҚ (жңҲвҶ’ж—ҘгҒ®гғ©гғғгғ—) гҒ®иІ еҖӨйҳІжӯўгҖӮ 1 гҒӨгҒ® switch гҒ§ 3 гӮұгғјгӮ№ (е№іе№ҙ/й–Ҹе№ҙ/1752) гӮ’зөұдёҖеҮҰзҗҶгҖӮ jan1() гҒҢгғҰгғӘгӮҰгӮ№вҶ’гӮ°гғ¬гӮҙгғӘгӮӘеҲҮжӣҝгӮ’еҶ…еҢ…гҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒ®гҒ§гҖҒ й–Ҹе№ҙеҲӨе®ҡгӮӮгҒқгҒ®жҒ©жҒөгӮ’еҸ—гҒ‘гҒҰиЈңжӯЈдёҚиҰҒгҖӮ 3 гғ–гғ©гғігғҒгҒ§йҖҡеёёгҒ® 10+ иЎҢгҒ®зү№ж®ҠгӮұгғјгӮ№еҮҰзҗҶгӮ’зҪ®гҒҚжҸӣгҒҲгӮӢгҖҒ K&R жөҒгҒ®ж•°еӯҰзҡ„гӮ»гғігӮ№гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: crypt.c's opening comment declares its lineage: 'A one-rotor machine designed along the lines of Enigma but considerably trivialized.' Original Enigma I (German WWII) had 3 rotors + plugboard + reflector, ~10^20 key space. Bell-32V's version: 1 rotor (t1) + reflector (t3, involution) + inverse rotor (t2 = inverse of t1), 256 elements per table (bytes, not 26 letters). The comment is unusually honest for cryptographic software: 'don't trust this with real secrets'. Both Morris & Thompson published cryptanalysis papers showing crypt is breakable in minutes with known-plaintext attack.
ai_analysis.human_story: 1918: Scherbius patents Enigma. 1939: Wehrmacht adopts it. 1940-43: Bletchley Park breaks it (Turing et al.). 1979: K&R-era Unix programmers reimplement a toy version in 91 lines of C as `crypt(1)`. 1980: Morris and Thompson publish the cryptanalysis. The opening comment 'considerably trivialized' is honest from the start вҖ” this was never meant to be strong. It's Enigma as teaching tool, Enigma as nostalgia, Enigma reduced to its essence: rotor + reflector + state machine. The 91-line homage to Bletchley Park.

appraiser_note: crypt.c гҒ®еҶ’й ӯгӮігғЎгғігғҲгҖҢ**A one-rotor machine designed along the lines of Enigma but considerably trivialized**гҖҚ гҒҢгҖҒ гҒ“гҒ®жҡ—еҸ·ж©ҹгҒ®иЎҖзөұгӮ’жҳҺзӨәгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ гӮӘгғӘгӮёгғҠгғ« Enigma I (гғҠгғҒгӮ№гғ»гғүгӮӨгғ„ WWII) гҒҜ 3 гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј + гғ—гғ©гӮ°гғңгғјгғү + еҸҚе°„жқҝгҖҒ йҚөз©әй–“зҙ„ 10^20гҖӮ Bell-32V зүҲгҒҜ 1 гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј (t1) + еҸҚе°„жқҝ (t3гҖҒ еҜҫеҗҲ) + йҖҶгғӯгғјгӮҝгғј (t2 = t1 гҒ®йҖҶ)гҖҒ 256 иҰҒзҙ гғҶгғјгғ–гғ« (гғҗгӮӨгғҲеҚҳдҪҚгҖҒ 26 ж–Үеӯ—гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„)гҖӮ жҡ—еҸ·гӮҪгғ•гғҲгӮҰгӮ§гӮўгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰгҒҜз•°дҫӢгҒ®жӯЈзӣҙгҒ•вҖ”вҖ”гҖҢгҒ“гӮҢгҒ§жң¬зү©гҒ®ж©ҹеҜҶгӮ’е®ҲгӮӢгҒӘгҖҚ гҒЁжңҖеҲқгҒӢгӮүе®ЈиЁҖгҖӮ Morris гҒЁ Thompson гҒҢи«–ж–ҮгҒ§гҖҢж—ўзҹҘе№іж–Үж”»ж’ғгҒ§ж•°еҲҶгҒ§з ҙгӮҢгӮӢгҖҚ гҒЁзӨәгҒ—гҒҹжҡ—еҸ·гҖӮ 91 иЎҢгҒ® Bletchley Park гҒёгҒ®гӮӘгғһгғјгӮёгғҘгҖҒ ж•ҷиӮІзӣ®зҡ„гҒ® Enigma зё®е°ҸзүҲгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Three permutation tables built in one loop: t1 (rotor, random permutation via Fisher-Yates shuffle), t3 (reflector, involution: t3[t3[i]] = i), and afterward t2 (inverse of t1). t1 generation: standard Fisher-Yates вҖ” swap t1[k] with t1[ic] where ic вҲҲ [0..k]. t3 generation: walk through unassigned positions, pair them up вҖ” `t3[k] = ic; t3[ic] = k;` creates a swap pair, ensuring t3 is its own inverse. PRNG: linear congruential `seed = 5*seed + buf[i%13]`, modulus 65521 (largest prime < 2^16). t2 derivation: `t2[t1[i]] = i` вҖ” exactly the inverse permutation. The mathematical structure (permutation + involution + inverse) recreates Enigma's reciprocal property: encryption == decryption with same key.
ai_analysis.human_story: Three tables, three mathematical objects. t1 is just a shuffle. t3 is special вҖ” it's an involution, meaning applying it twice returns the input. That's what makes Enigma's reflector work: light enters, bounces back through the rotors in reverse, and the operator can read the result. t2 = t1вҒ»В№ closes the loop. The Fisher-Yates shuffle is Knuth's textbook algorithm, but the pairing-up for t3 is bespoke: find an unassigned position, find another unassigned position, marry them. 17 lines of code build the entire mathematical machinery of a rotor cipher.

appraiser_note: 3 гҒӨгҒ®й ҶеҲ—гғҶгғјгғ–гғ«гӮ’ 1 гғ«гғјгғ—гҒ§ж§ӢзҜүгҖӮ **t1** (гғӯгғјгӮҝгғјгҖҒ Fisher-Yates гӮ·гғЈгғғгғ•гғ«гҒ§е®Ңе…Ёгғ©гғігғҖгғ й ҶеҲ—)гҖҒ **t3** (еҸҚе°„жқҝгҖҒ еҜҫеҗҲ = `t3[t3[i]] = i`)гҖҒ гҒқгҒ—гҒҰеҫҢгҒ§ **t2** (t1 гҒ®йҖҶй ҶеҲ—)гҖӮ t1: жЁҷжә– Fisher-YatesвҖ”вҖ”`t1[k]` гҒЁ `t1[ic]` (ic вҲҲ [0..k]) гӮ’ swapгҖӮ t3: жңӘеүІгӮҠеҪ“гҒҰдҪҚзҪ®гӮ’й ҶгҒ«иө°жҹ»гҒ—гҒҰ **гғҡгӮўеҢ–**вҖ”вҖ”`t3[k] = ic; t3[ic] = k;` гҒ§ k гҒЁ ic гӮ’зӣёдә’гҒ«еҜҫеҝңгҒ•гҒӣгҖҒ иҮӘе·ұйҖҶе…ғгӮ’ж§ӢзҜүгҖӮ PRNG гҒҜз·ҡеҪўеҗҲеҗҢжі• `seed = 5*seed + buf[i%13]`гҖҒ modulus 65521 (2^16 жңӘжәҖгҒ®жңҖеӨ§зҙ ж•°)гҖӮ t2 = t1 гҒ®йҖҶ: `t2[t1[i]] = i`гҖӮ гҒ“гҒ®ж•°еӯҰзҡ„ж§ӢйҖ  (й ҶеҲ— + еҜҫеҗҲ + йҖҶй ҶеҲ—) гҒҢ Enigma гҒ® **жҡ—еҸ·еҢ– == еҫ©еҸ·** гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶдә’жҸӣжҖ§гӮ’е®ҹзҸҫгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ 17 иЎҢгҒ§гғӯгғјгӮҝгғјжҡ—еҸ·ж©ҹгҒ®е…Ёж©ҹж§ӢгӮ’ж§ӢзҜүгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Textbook fork-pipe-exec pattern: pipe() creates parent-child channel, fork() spawns child, child closes stdin/stdout and dup()s the pipe ends to fd 0/1, then execl()s /usr/lib/makekey (with /lib/makekey as fallback for V7 vs Bell-32V layout). Parent writes 10 bytes (8 password + 2 wrap chars), waits for child, reads 13 bytes back as the actual key. The line `while (*pw) *pw++ = '\0';` zeros the password in argv[1] in-memory вҖ” so `ps` no longer shows it, core dumps won't leak it. The same idea is now `explicit_bzero(3)` in modern systems. Two execl() calls in a row: first one succeeds вҶ’ returns control means fail вҶ’ try second. exec doesn't return on success.
ai_analysis.human_story: Two textbook patterns in 18 lines. First, fork-pipe-execl: every Unix programming book teaches this. Bell-32V's crypt is one of the early examples. Second, password scrubbing: `while (*pw) *pw++ = '\0';` overwrites the password byte in process memory so it can't leak via ps, core dumps, swap. 1979's programmers were already thinking about memory hygiene вҖ” what modern hardening calls 'sensitive data clearing'. The 3-character idiom became `explicit_bzero(3)` in OpenBSD, `SecureZeroMemory` in Windows, `OPENSSL_cleanse` in OpenSSL. The instinct is older than the helper functions.

appraiser_note: ж•ҷз§‘жӣёзҡ„гҒӘ fork-pipe-exec гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғіпјҡ`pipe()` гҒ§иҰӘеӯҗй–“гғҒгғЈгғҚгғ«гҖҒ `fork()` гҒ§еӯҗеҲҶеІҗгҖҒ еӯҗеҒҙгҒ§ stdin/stdout гӮ’ close + dup гҒ§гғ‘гӮӨгғ—з«ҜгҒ«зҪ®жҸӣгҖҒ `execl("/usr/lib/makekey", "-", 0)` гҒ§ makekey иө·еӢ• (`/lib/makekey` гғ•гӮ©гғјгғ«гғҗгғғгӮҜд»ҳгҒҚгҖҒ V7 гҒЁ Bell-32V гҒ®гғ¬гӮӨгӮўгӮҰгғҲе·®еҗёеҸҺ)гҖӮ иҰӘгҒҜ 10 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ (8 гғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғү + 2 гғ©гғғгғ—ж–Үеӯ—) жӣёгҒҚгҖҒ waitгҖҒ 13 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгҒ®йҚөгӮ’иӘӯгҒҝжҲ»гҒҷгҖӮ `while (*pw) *pw++ = '\0';` гҒ® 1 иЎҢгҒҢ argv[1] гҒ®гғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүгӮ’ **гғЎгғўгғӘгӮјгғӯгӮҜгғӘгӮў**вҖ”вҖ”`ps -ef` гҒ§гғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүгҒҢиҰӢгҒҲгҒӘгҒҸгҒӘгӮҠгҖҒ core dump гҒ«гӮӮж®ӢгӮүгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ зҸҫд»ЈгҒ® `explicit_bzero(3)` (OpenBSD)гҖҒ `SecureZeroMemory` (Windows)гҖҒ `OPENSSL_cleanse` гҒ®зҘ–гҖӮ 2 гҒӨгҒ® `execl` гҒҢдёҰгҒ¶гҒ®гҒҜгҖҢжңҖеҲқгҒҢжҲҗеҠҹгҒӘгӮүжҲ»гҒЈгҒҰгҒ“гҒӘгҒ„гҖҒ еӨұж•—жҷӮгҒ®гҒҝж¬ЎгҒёгҖҚ гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶ Unix гҒ®дҪңжі•гҖӮ 18 иЎҢгҒ« Unix ж•ҷз§‘жӣёгҒ®дё»иҰҒгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғі 2 гҒӨгҒҢеҮқзё®гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: The cipher's heart: one line that recreates Enigma's signal path. Read inside-out: (i+n1) shifts by rotor1 position, t1[] passes through rotor1 forward, +n2 shifts by rotor2 position, t3[] hits the reflector (involution), -n2 unshifts rotor2, t2[] passes through rotor1 inverse, -n1 unshifts rotor1. All & MASK to wrap mod 256. Then n1++ (right rotor advances per char), and when n1 wraps n2++ (middle rotor advances). The involution property of t3 makes encryption and decryption identical operations with the same key вҖ” `crypt < a > b; crypt < b > c` yields c == a. Unlike real Enigma's stepping mechanism (which had irregular notch positions), this stepping is fully predictable from key, contributing to its cryptanalytic weakness.
ai_analysis.human_story: Six function calls, one C expression. Light enters Enigma at the keyboard, traverses the input wheel, passes through three rotors, hits the reflector, returns through the three rotors backward, exits through the output wheel. Bell-32V compresses this to: t2[t3[t1[i+n1]+n2]-n2]-n1. The reflector (t3) is the magic вҖ” applying the whole expression twice with the same key returns the original input. Encryption is self-inverse. This is why Enigma operators could decrypt with the same machine setting they used to encrypt. The 1979 C line preserves that fundamental property in 60 characters.

appraiser_note: жҡ—еҸ·ж©ҹгҒ®еҝғиҮ“пјҡ1 иЎҢгҒ§ Enigma гҒ®дҝЎеҸ·зөҢи·ҜгӮ’еҶҚж§ӢзҜүгҖӮ еҶ…еҒҙгҒӢгӮүеӨ–еҒҙгҒё: `(i+n1)` гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј 1 дҪҚзҪ®гҒ§гӮ·гғ•гғҲгҖҒ `t1[]` гҒ§й Ҷж–№еҗ‘гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј 1 йҖҡйҒҺгҖҒ `+n2` гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј 2 дҪҚзҪ®гӮ·гғ•гғҲгҖҒ `t3[]` гҒ§еҸҚе°„жқҝ (еҜҫеҗҲ)гҖҒ `-n2` гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј 2 жҲ»гҒ—гҖҒ `t2[]` гҒ§гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј 1 йҖҶж–№еҗ‘гҖҒ `-n1` гҒ§гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј 1 жҲ»гҒ—гҖӮ гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰ `& MASK` гҒ§ mod 256 гғ©гғғгғ—гҖӮ n1++ (еҸігғӯгғјгӮҝгғјгҖҒ 1 ж–Үеӯ—гҒ”гҒЁйҖІиЎҢ)гҖҒ n1 гғ©гғғгғ—жҷӮ n2++ (дёӯеӨ®гғӯгғјгӮҝгғј)гҖӮ t3 гҒҢеҜҫеҗҲгҒӘгҒ®гҒ§жҡ—еҸ·еҢ–гҒЁеҫ©еҸ·гҒҢ **еҗҢгҒҳж“ҚдҪң**вҖ”вҖ”`crypt < a > b; crypt < b > c` гҒ§ c == aгҖӮ жң¬зү© Enigma гҒ® stepping mechanism (дёҚиҰҸеүҮгғҺгғғгғҒдҪҚзҪ®) гҒЁз•°гҒӘгӮҠгҖҒ гҒ“гҒ®йҖІиЎҢгҒҜйҚөгҒӢгӮүе®Ңе…ЁдәҲжё¬еҸҜиғҪгҒ§гҖҒ гҒ“гӮҢгҒҢжҡ—еҸ·и§Јжһҗзҡ„ејұгҒ•гҒ®дёҖеӣ гҖӮ 6 гҒӨгҒ®й–ўж•°е‘јгҒіеҮәгҒ—гӮ’ 60 ж–Үеӯ—гҒ® C ејҸгҒ«ең§зё®гҖҒ Bletchley Park зё®еӣігҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: dc represents arbitrary-precision numbers as byte arrays where each byte holds a base-100 digit (0-99). struct blk has 4 pointers: beg (buffer start), last (buffer end / capacity), rd (read cursor), wt (write cursor). Operations modeled as stdio-style: sgetc/sputc/sfeof/sfbeg/rewind вҖ” number manipulation feels like file I/O. Base-100 chosen for the sweet spot: maximum decimal digits per byte (2) while keeping addition's `a + b + carry` within unsigned char range (< 256). 1234567890 stored as [12, 34, 56, 78, 90] in 5 bytes. Addition is exactly grade-school carry arithmetic, just in base 100.
ai_analysis.human_story: Pick your base wisely. Base 10 wastes a byte per digit. Base 256 (binary) is fast but hard to print decimally. Base 100 hits the middle: 2 decimal digits per byte, addition still fits in unsigned char, output to decimal is two %02d printfs. 1979's dc chose this carefully вҖ” read each byte, print 2 digits. The struct blk type with rd/wt/beg/last mimics FILE*, making C programmers feel at home with arbitrary-precision arithmetic. Python's int, JavaScript BigInt, Java BigInteger вҖ” all eventually do similar tricks. dc was here first.

appraiser_note: dc гҒҜд»»ж„ҸзІҫеәҰж•°гӮ’ **зҷҫйҖІжі•гғҗгӮӨгғҲй…ҚеҲ—**гҒ§иЎЁзҸҫвҖ”вҖ”еҗ„гғҗгӮӨгғҲгҒҢ 0гҖң99 гҒ®гҖҢзҷҫйҖІжі• 1 жЎҒгҖҚгҖҒ `struct blk` гҒҢ beg/last (гғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎеўғз•Ң) + rd/wt (зҸҫеңЁгҒ®гӮ«гғјгӮҪгғ«) гҒ® 4 гғқгӮӨгғігӮҝгҖӮ ж“ҚдҪңгҒҜ stdio йўЁ: `sgetc`/`sputc`/`sfeof`/`sfbeg`/`rewind`вҖ”вҖ”ж•°еҖӨж“ҚдҪңгҒҢгғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ« I/O гҒ®гӮҲгҒҶгҒ«жүұгҒҲгӮӢгҖӮ зҷҫйҖІжі•йҒёе®ҡгҒ®зҗҶз”ұпјҡ1 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгҒӮгҒҹгӮҠжңҖеӨ§гҒ®еҚҒйҖІжғ…е ұйҮҸ (2 жЎҒ) + еҠ з®— `a + b + carry` гҒҢ unsigned char зҜ„еӣІеҶ… (< 256) гҒ§жёҲгӮҖгҖӮ 1234567890 вҶ’ [12, 34, 56, 78, 90] гҒ® 5 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгҖӮ еҠ з®—гҒҜдәәй–“гҒ®зӯҶз®—гҒЁе®Ңе…ЁеҗҢгҒҳжүӢй ҶгҖҒ гҒҹгҒ гҒ—еҹәеә• 100гҖӮ Python intгҖҒ JavaScript BigIntгҖҒ Java BigInteger гҒ®зҘ–гҖҒ dc гҒҢжңҖеҲқгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: commnds() is the heart of dc: a typical REPL with `while(1) { c = readc(); switch(c) { ... } }`. 60+ case branches, one character = one operation. RPN (Reverse Polish Notation): digits push to stack, operators pop and compute. Case-letter conventions: lowercase/uppercase pairs indicate variations (s vs S = without-pop vs with-pop on register save, p vs P = with/without newline, q vs Q = single quit vs multi-level quit). Symbols carry semantic baggage: ? = read line (HP calculator), ! = shell escape (vi/ed/mail tradition), ^ = exponent (math notation), v = sqrt (single-letter mnemonic). The single-character commands echo HP-35 (1972) calculator's button layout.
ai_analysis.human_story: RPN was popularized by Hewlett-Packard's HP-35 calculator in 1972 вҖ” engineers loved it. By 1979, dc brought RPN to Unix command line. One character per operation: type `2 3 +` for addition. The switch statement has 60+ cases because dc covers more than arithmetic вҖ” print, duplicate, register save/load, macro execute, conditional execute. The uppercase/lowercase pairs are clever ergonomics: `s` saves without popping (peek-style), `S` saves and pops (consuming). Same letter, two ergonomic variations. RPN survived as Forth, PostScript, calculator keyboards вҖ” but dc was its early Unix home.

appraiser_note: commnds() гҒҜ dc гҒ®еҝғиҮ“вҖ”вҖ”е…ёеһӢзҡ„ REPL: `while(1) { c = readc(); switch(c) { ... } }`гҖҒ 60+ гӮұгғјгӮ№гҖҒ 1 ж–Үеӯ— = 1 еӢ•дҪңгҖӮ RPN (йҖҶгғқгғјгғ©гғігғүиЁҳжі•): ж•°еӯ—гҒҜгӮ№гӮҝгғғгӮҜгҒ« pushгҖҒ жј”з®—еӯҗгҒҜ pop + иЁҲз®— + pushгҖӮ еӨ§ж–Үеӯ—/е°Ҹж–Үеӯ—еҜҫгҒ®иҰҸзҙ„: `s` (save without pop) vs `S` (save with pop)гҖҒ `p` (with newline) vs `P` (no newline)гҖҒ `q` (quit) vs `Q` (multi-quit)гҖӮ иЁҳеҸ·гҒ®ж„Ҹе‘іи«–: `?` = readline (HP йӣ»еҚ“з”ұжқҘ)гҖҒ `!` = shell escape (vi/ed/mail ж…Јзҝ’)гҖҒ `^` = exponent (ж•°еӯҰиЁҳжі•)гҖҒ `v` = sqrt (й ӯж–Үеӯ—)гҖӮ HP-35 йӣ»еҚ“ (1972) гҒ®гғңгӮҝгғій…ҚзҪ®гҒ®зі»иӯңвҖ”вҖ”RPN ж–ҮеҢ–гӮ’ Unix command line гҒёжҢҒгҒЎиҫјгӮ“гҒ гҖӮ ForthгҖҒ PostScriptгҖҒ йӣ»еҚ“гӮӯгғјгғңгғјгғүгҒ®зҘ–гҒ гҒҢгҖҒ dc гҒҢж—©жңҹ Unix гҒ®жң¬жӢ ең°гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Macro feature: `[` reads characters until matching `]`, builds a string, pushes to stack. Nesting handled by counter n. `x` pops the string and pushes it onto readstk (input source stack) вҖ” subsequent reads come from the macro body. Returns when readstk pops. Combined with 256 registers (stable[TBLSZ]) accessed via S/L (save/load), and `<`/`>`/`=` conditional execute (compare top two, execute named register if true), dc becomes Turing complete. Example factorial: `[d 1 - d 1 <e * lFx] sF` defines F(n) = if n>1 then n*F(n-1). Stack-based control flow predates Forth (1968) by no clear margin; dc's macros (V1, 1971) may be earlier. Register stable[] is per-register linked list (S/L is push/pop, s/l is set/peek) вҖ” registers are themselves stacks.
ai_analysis.human_story: dc isn't just a calculator. With `[...]` string literals, register save/load via S/L, conditional execute via <a, dc becomes a Turing-complete language. Write a factorial macro, push 5, execute вҖ” get 120. Write a prime tester macro, push numbers, get truth values. The combination of: (1) stack for arithmetic, (2) registers for variables, (3) macros for functions, (4) conditional execute for control flow gives you a programming language in 1979. It's Forth before Forth was famous. PostScript later took this same stack-based model and made it the language of laser printers.

appraiser_note: гғһгӮҜгғӯж©ҹиғҪ: `[` гҒ§ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—иӘӯгҒҝе§ӢгӮҒгҖҒ еҜҫеҝңгҒҷгӮӢ `]` гҒҫгҒ§гӮ’ 1 гҒӨгҒ®ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰгӮ№гӮҝгғғгӮҜ push (nesting гӮ«гӮҰгғігӮҝ n гҒ§ `[[nested]]` гӮӮжӯЈгҒ—гҒҸеҮҰзҗҶ)гҖӮ `x` гҒ§ pop гҒ—гҒҹж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮ’ readstk (е…ҘеҠӣгӮҪгғјгӮ№гӮ№гӮҝгғғгӮҜ) гҒ« pushвҖ”вҖ”д»ҘйҷҚгҒ® readc() гҒҜгғһгӮҜгғӯжң¬дҪ“гҒӢгӮүиӘӯгҒҝеҮәгҒҷгҖҒ зөӮдәҶжҷӮгҒ« readstk гӮ’ pop гҒ—гҒҰе…ғгҒ«жҲ»гӮӢгҖӮ 256 гғ¬гӮёгӮ№гӮҝ (stable[TBLSZ]) гҒ« `S`/`L` (save/load) гҒ§гӮўгӮҜгӮ»гӮ№гҖҒ `<`/`>`/`=` гҒ§гҖҢtop гҒЁж¬ЎгҒ®еҖӨгӮ’жҜ”ијғгҖҒ зңҹгҒӘгӮүжҢҮе®ҡгғ¬гӮёгӮ№гӮҝгӮ’ executeгҖҚ гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶжқЎд»¶еҲҶеІҗгҖӮ гҒ“гӮҢгҒ§ dc гҒҜ **Turing е®ҢеӮҷ**гҖӮ йҡҺд№—гғһгӮҜгғӯ: `[d 1 - d 1 <e * lFx] sF` гҒ§ F(n) = if n>1 then n*F(n-1)гҖӮ (1) з®—иЎ“з”ЁгӮ№гӮҝгғғгӮҜгҖҒ (2) еӨүж•°з”Ёгғ¬гӮёгӮ№гӮҝгҖҒ (3) й–ўж•°з”ЁгғһгӮҜгғӯгҖҒ (4) жқЎд»¶е®ҹиЎҢвҖ”вҖ”гғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғ иЁҖиӘһгҒ®е®Ңе…ЁгҒӘж§ӢжҲҗиҰҒзҙ гӮ’ 1979 е№ҙгҒ«жҸҗдҫӣгҖӮ Forth ж–ҮеҢ–гҖҒ PostScript гҒ®зҘ–гҖӮ

#ifdef interdata
#define NEGBYTE 0200
#define MASK (-1 & ~0377)
#define sgetc(p)  ( ((p)->rd==(p)->wt) ? EOF :( ((*(p)->rd & NEGBYTE) != 0) ? ( *(p)->rd++ | MASK): *(p)->rd++ ))
#define slookc(p) ( ((p)->rd==(p)->wt) ? EOF :( ((*(p)->rd & NEGBYTE) != 0) ? (*(p)->rd | MASK) : *(p)->rd ))
#define sbackc(p) ( ((p)->rd==(p)->beg) ? EOF :( ((*(--(p)->rd) & NEGBYTE) != 0) ? (*(p)->rd | MASK): *(p)->rd ))
#endif
#ifndef interdata
#define sgetc(p)  (((p)->rd==(p)->wt)?EOF:*(p)->rd++)
#define slookc(p) (((p)->rd==(p)->wt)?EOF:*(p)->rd)
#define sbackc(p) (((p)->rd==(p)->beg)?EOF:*(--(p)->rd))
#endif

ai_analysis.technical_context: Interdata 8/32 (Perkin-Elmer mini, 1970s) вҖ” the platform of the first non-Bell Unix port (Wollongong University, Australia, 1977). Its C compiler zero-extended `char` to `int` instead of sign-extending (PDP-11/VAX behavior). dc.h's #ifdef interdata defines sgetc/slookc/sbackc to manually sign-extend when the high bit is set: check `*(p)->rd & NEGBYTE` (0x80), if true OR with MASK (-1 & ~0377 = upper bits all 1) to fill in the sign bits. The else branch (PDP-11/VAX) just does `*(p)->rd++` because automatic sign-extension takes care of it. 4 lines of macros encapsulate a major architectural difference. The 'one Unix source, many architectures' DNA in action.
ai_analysis.human_story: Interdata 8/32 was Unix's first journey outside Bell Labs and Berkeley вҖ” Wollongong University, Australia, 1977. Their C compiler treated `char` as unsigned (zero-extension to int), unlike PDP-11/VAX (signed, sign-extension). One macro choice could break arithmetic on negative numbers. dc.h's solution: hand-write the sign extension via NEGBYTE check + OR with MASK. Four #ifdef macros encode the architectural divergence so the rest of dc.c can use sgetc() ignoring the difference. This style вҖ” encapsulate platform quirks in macros, write the algorithm once вҖ” is the seed of every cross-platform C codebase: Linux, macOS, AIX, Windows-Cygwin, all running the same source files with #ifdef gates.

appraiser_note: Interdata 8/32 (Perkin-Elmer иЈҪ miniгҖҒ 1970 е№ҙд»Ј) гҒҜ Bell/Berkeley д»ҘеӨ–гҒ®жңҖеҲқгҒ® Unix з§»жӨҚе…Ҳ (Wollongong еӨ§еӯҰгӮӘгғјгӮ№гғҲгғ©гғӘгӮўгҖҒ 1977)гҖӮ гҒқгҒ® C гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ©гҒҜ `char` вҶ’ `int` гҒ®жҳҮж јгӮ’ **гӮјгғӯжӢЎејө**гҒ§иЎҢгҒЈгҒҹвҖ”вҖ”PDP-11/VAX гҒҜз¬ҰеҸ·жӢЎејөгҖӮ dc.h гҒҜ `#ifdef interdata` гҒ§ **жүӢеӢ•з¬ҰеҸ·жӢЎејө**гӮ’ sgetc/slookc/sbackc гҒ«зө„гҒҝиҫјгӮҖ: `*(p)->rd & NEGBYTE` (0x80) гҒ§жңҖдёҠдҪҚгғ“гғғгғҲжӨңжҹ»гҖҒ з«ӢгҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гӮҢгҒ° `| MASK` (-1 & ~0377 = дёҠдҪҚгғ“гғғгғҲе…Ё 1) гҒ§з¬ҰеҸ·жӢЎејөгӮ’иЈңгҒҶгҖӮ else зҜҖ (PDP-11/VAX) гҒҜиҮӘеӢ•з¬ҰеҸ·жӢЎејөгҒ«д»»гҒӣгҒҰзҙ жңҙгҒӘ `*(p)->rd++`гҖӮ 4 иЎҢгҒ®гғһгӮҜгғӯгҒ§гӮўгғјгӮӯгғҶгӮҜгғҒгғЈгҒ®е·®гӮ’еҗёеҸҺгҒ—гҖҒ dc.c жң¬дҪ“гҒҜ `sgetc()` гӮ’ж„ҸиӯҳгҒӣгҒҡдҪҝгҒҲгӮӢгҖӮ гҖҢ**гғ—гғ©гғғгғҲгғ•гӮ©гғјгғ е·®гӮ’гғһгӮҜгғӯгҒ«й–үгҒҳиҫјгӮҒгҒҰгҖҒ гӮўгғ«гӮҙгғӘгӮәгғ гҒҜ 1 еәҰжӣёгҒҸ**гҖҚвҖ”вҖ”Linux/macOS/AIX/Cygwin гҒҢеҗҢгҒҳгӮҪгғјгӮ№гҒӢгӮү build гҒ§гҒҚгӮӢ DNA гҒ®иө·жәҗгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: init's main is 10 lines but defines Unix boot sequence. #define EVER ;; lets `for(EVER)` read 'forever' вҖ” K&R aesthetic. Five-stage cycle: shutdown() kills all processes, single() forks /bin/sh for single-user maintenance, runcom() executes /etc/rc startup script, merge() reads /etc/ttys and spawns getty per tty, multiple() loops on wait() to respawn dead gettys. Normally multiple() never returns (always children to wait on). The for(EVER) reloop happens only via SIGHUP вҶ’ reset() вҶ’ longjmp(sjbuf, 1) вҶ’ setjmp returns and main re-enters. PID 1 cannot exit: kernel panic if init dies. Modern Linux: 'Kernel panic - not syncing: Attempted to kill init!'. 50 years of OS architecture flow from this 10-line main.
ai_analysis.human_story: Ten lines for the most important process in Unix. PID 1. Parent of all. Cannot die. The for(EVER) macro is K&R cuteness вҖ” `for(;;)` written as 'forever'. The 5-stage cycle is the Unix boot story: kill, single-user, run startup script, spawn login terminals, watch them. Every modern init system вҖ” System V init, BSD init, systemd, launchd, OpenRC, runit, s6 вҖ” implements these same 5 phases, just with vastly more configuration. The 1979 init.c is a kernel-blessed user-space miracle: alone in the address space, eternal, parent to everyone.

appraiser_note: init гҒ® main гҒҜ **гӮҸгҒҡгҒӢ 10 иЎҢ**гҒ§ Unix гҒ®иө·еӢ•гӮ·гғјгӮұгғігӮ№е…ЁдҪ“гӮ’е®ҡзҫ©гҖӮ `#define EVER ;;` гҒ§ `for(EVER)` гӮ’гҖҢforeverгҖҚ гҒЁиӘӯгҒҫгҒӣгӮӢ K&R зҫҺеӯҰгҖӮ 5 гӮ№гғҶгғјгӮёгӮөгӮӨгӮҜгғ«: `shutdown()` е…Ёгғ—гғӯгӮ»гӮ№ killгҖҒ `single()` гҒ§ /bin/sh fork (гӮ·гғігӮ°гғ«гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғј)гҖҒ `runcom()` гҒ§ /etc/rc е®ҹиЎҢ (иө·еӢ•гӮ№гӮҜгғӘгғ—гғҲ)гҖҒ `merge()` гҒ§ /etc/ttys иӘӯгҒҝ + еҗ„ tty гҒ« getty forkгҖҒ `multiple()` гҒ§ wait() гғ«гғјгғ— (respawn)гҖӮ йҖҡеёё multiple() гҒҜжҲ»гӮүгҒӘгҒ„ (еёёгҒ« wait еҜҫиұЎгҒӮгӮҠ)вҖ”вҖ”for(EVER) гғ«гғјгғ—гҒҜ SIGHUP вҶ’ reset() вҶ’ longjmp(sjbuf, 1) вҶ’ setjmp жҲ»гӮҠ вҶ’ main еҶҚйҖІе…ҘгҒ§еӣһгӮӢгҖӮ PID 1 гҒҜ exit гҒ§гҒҚгҒӘгҒ„: kernel гҒҢ panic гҒҷгӮӢ (зҸҫд»Ј Linux: гҖҢKernel panic - not syncing: Attempted to kill init!гҖҚ)гҖӮ 50 е№ҙгҒ® OS гӮўгғјгӮӯгғҶгӮҜгғҒгғЈгҒҢгҒ“гҒ® 10 иЎҢгҒӢгӮүжөҒгӮҢеҮәгӮӢвҖ”вҖ”SysV init/BSD init/systemd/launchd/OpenRC/runit/s6 гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰгҒ®зҘ–гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: multiple() invents 'respawn' вҖ” the automatic restart of dead processes. Loop: wait() blocks for any child to die, gets that PID, scans itab[] for matching entry, calls rmut() to log the logout in utmp/wtmp, then dfork() to spawn a fresh getty on that tty. A getty that times out, crashes, or completes login flow gets immediately replaced by a new one. wait() == -1 (ECHILD, no children) returns from multiple(), letting main proceed (this happens after SIGHUP reset kills everything). The respawn keyword later became declarative in /etc/inittab (System V): `1:23:respawn:/sbin/getty 9600 tty1`. systemd's Restart=always, supervisord's autorestart=true, Kubernetes restartPolicy=Always вҖ” all descendants of this 14-line function.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why does your terminal always show `login:` after you log out? Because of these 14 lines from 1979. getty dies вҶ’ init's wait() returns вҶ’ rmut() records the logout вҶ’ dfork() spawns a new getty вҶ’ `login:` reappears. The pattern вҖ” wait, identify, restart вҖ” is the seed of every supervisor system in Unix: System V's respawn, BSD's getty respawn, monit, supervisord, runit's chpst, daemontools, systemd unit files, Kubernetes pod restart policies. 'Self-healing' as a software pattern was invented here, in 14 lines.

appraiser_note: multiple() гҒҜ **respawn** (жӯ»гӮ“гҒ гғ—гғӯгӮ»гӮ№гҒ®иҮӘеӢ•еҶҚиө·еӢ•) гӮ’зҷәжҳҺгҒ—гҒҹ 14 иЎҢгҖӮ гғ«гғјгғ—: wait() гҒ§д»»ж„ҸгҒ®еӯҗгғ—гғӯгӮ»гӮ№гҒ®жӯ»гӮ’еҫ…гҒӨ вҶ’ жӯ»гӮ“гҒ  PID гӮ’еҸ–еҫ— вҶ’ itab[] гӮ’иө°жҹ»гҒ—гҒҰдёҖиҮҙгҒҷгӮӢгӮЁгғігғҲгғӘгӮ’зҷәиҰӢ вҶ’ rmut() гҒ§гғӯгӮ°гӮўгӮҰгғҲгӮ’ utmp/wtmp гҒ«иЁҳйҢІ вҶ’ dfork() гҒ§ж–°гҒ—гҒ„ getty гӮ’иө·еӢ•гҖӮ getty гҒҢгӮҝгӮӨгғ гӮўгӮҰгғҲгҖҒ гӮҜгғ©гғғгӮ·гғҘгҖҒ login е®ҢдәҶгҒ§ж¶ҲгҒҲгҒҰгӮӮгҖҒ еҚіеә§гҒ«ж–°гҒ—гҒ„ getty гҒ«зҪ®гҒҚжҸӣгӮҸгӮӢгҖӮ wait() == -1 (ECHILDгҖҒ еӯҗгҒӘгҒ—) гҒ§ multiple() returnгҖҒ main гҒёжҲ»гӮӢ (SIGHUP reset гҒ§е…Ёгғ—гғӯгӮ»гӮ№ж¶ҲгҒҲгҒҹеҫҢ)гҖӮ еҫҢгҒ« SysV init гҒ§е®ЈиЁҖзҡ„гҒ«: `1:23:respawn:/sbin/getty 9600 tty1`гҖӮ systemd `Restart=always`гҖҒ supervisord `autorestart=true`гҖҒ Kubernetes `restartPolicy=Always`вҖ”вҖ”е…ЁйғЁгҒ“гҒ® 14 иЎҢгҒ®еӯҗеӯ«гҖӮ гҖҢself-healingгҖҚ гӮҪгғ•гғҲгӮҰгӮ§гӮўгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒ®иө·жәҗгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: dfork: fork + tty setup + exec getty. Child resets signal handlers (parent ignored SIGHUP/SIGINT for safety), constructs /dev/ttyXX path via maktty(), then carefully resets terminal ownership: chown(tty, 0, 0) = root:root, chmod(tty, 0622) = rw--w--w- (owner read/write, others write only). The 0622 permission is brilliant: lets `write` and `wall` send messages to this terminal (write needed) but prevents eavesdropping (no read for others). open(tty, 2) opens fd 0, dup(0); dup(0); duplicates to fd 1 and fd 2 вҖ” establishes stdin/stdout/stderr convention. Then execl(getty, '-', 'tty00', 0): '-' marks login shell, second arg is the tty name passed to getty.
ai_analysis.human_story: Every tty resets to fresh state when init spawns a new getty: ownership becomes root, permissions become 0622 (rw--w--w-). The 0622 magic enables `write user@host` and `wall hello` to broadcast messages while preventing unauthorized snooping. Then fd 0/1/2 all point at the tty вҖ” the stdin/stdout/stderr convention is BORN here. Before exec, signal handlers are reset because the parent (init) had them ignored. The 18-line dfork is the canonical 'how to spawn a setuid-aware terminal-attached child' pattern that every Unix shell, ssh-server, container runtime quietly imitates.

appraiser_note: dfork гҒҜ fork + tty еҲқжңҹеҢ– + getty иө·еӢ•гҖӮ еӯҗеҒҙ: parent (init) гҒҢ ignore гҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒҹ SIGHUP/SIGINT гӮ’гғҮгғ•гӮ©гғ«гғҲгҒ«жҲ»гҒҷгҖҒ maktty() гҒ§ /dev/ttyXX гғ‘гӮ№ж§ӢзҜүгҖҒ **`chown(tty, 0, 0)`** гҒ§ root:rootгҖҒ **`chmod(tty, 0622)` гҒ§ `rw--w--w-`** (жүҖжңүиҖ… rwгҖҒ д»–иҖ… w гҒ®гҒҝ)гҖӮ 0622 гҒҢз§ҖйҖёвҖ”вҖ”`write` гӮ„ `wall` гҒ§д»–дәәгҒёгғЎгғғгӮ»гғјгӮёйҖҒдҝЎеҸҜиғҪ (write иЁұеҸҜ)гҖҒ гҒ§гӮӮзӣ—гҒҝиӘӯгҒҝгҒҜдёҚеҸҜ (read дёҚиЁұеҸҜ)гҖӮ `open(tty, 2)` гҒ§ fd 0гҖҒ `dup(0); dup(0);` гҒ§ fd 1 гҒЁ fd 2 гҒ«гӮӮиӨҮиЈҪвҖ”вҖ”**stdin/stdout/stderr ж…Јзҝ’гҒ®иө·жәҗ**гҖӮ `execl(getty, "-", "tty00", 0)` гҒ§ getty иө·еӢ•гҖҒ "-" гҒҜ login shell гғһгғјгӮҜгҖӮ гҖҢsetuid еҜҫеҝң + terminal жҺҘз¶ҡеӯҗгғ—гғӯгӮ»гӮ№иө·еӢ•гҖҚ гҒ®жӯЈжә–гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҖҒ shell/ssh-server/container runtime гҒ®гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰгҒҢеҶ…йғЁгҒ§жЁЎеҖЈгҒҷгӮӢ 18 иЎҢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: init uses setjmp/longjmp as exception handling for signal-driven reload. setjmp(sjbuf) at main's start saves the stack/PC state. signal(SIGHUP, reset) installs reset() as SIGHUP handler. When admin runs `kill -HUP 1`, kernel invokes reset() which does longjmp(sjbuf, 1) вҖ” non-local jump back to setjmp regardless of call depth. setjmp returns 1 (vs 0 first time), main re-enters the for(EVER) cycle, killing all gettys and re-reading /etc/ttys. This launched the 'SIGHUP = reload config' convention: sendmail, nginx, inetd, cron, syslogd, dnsmasq all use SIGHUP for config reload. setjmp/longjmp itself became the C-level exception primitive вҖ” used in libpng error recovery, OCaml runtime, Common Lisp catch/throw analogs.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you reload a daemon's config without restarting? In 1979, init.c invented the pattern: SIGHUP triggers a handler that longjmps back to the start of main. The setjmp/longjmp idiom is C's only built-in non-local exit вҖ” no exceptions, no goto across functions, just this register-saving primitive. Every major daemon adopted SIGHUP-reloads: sendmail rereads sendmail.cf, nginx rereads nginx.conf, syslogd reopens log files (logrotate's friend), cron rereads crontabs. Type `kill -HUP <pid>` today and you're invoking a convention started by 5 lines in 1979's init.c. The reset() function is 1 line: longjmp(sjbuf, 1). The entire 'reload config' design pattern in 1 line.

appraiser_note: init гҒҜ setjmp/longjmp гӮ’ **гӮ·гӮ°гғҠгғ«й§ҶеӢ•гғӘгғӯгғјгғүгҒ®дҫӢеӨ–еҮҰзҗҶ**гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰдҪҝгҒҶгҖӮ main гҒ®еҶ’й ӯгҒ§ `setjmp(sjbuf)` гҒҢгӮ№гӮҝгғғгӮҜ/PC зҠ¶ж…ӢгӮ’дҝқеӯҳгҖҒ `signal(SIGHUP, reset)` гҒ§ SIGHUP гғҸгғігғүгғ©гҒ« reset() гӮ’иЁӯе®ҡгҖӮ з®ЎзҗҶиҖ…гҒҢ `kill -HUP 1` е®ҹиЎҢ вҶ’ kernel гҒҢ reset() гӮ’е‘јгҒ¶ вҶ’ reset() гҒҢ `longjmp(sjbuf, 1)` гҒ§ main гҒ® setjmp ең°зӮ№гҒё **йқһгғӯгғјгӮ«гғ«гӮёгғЈгғігғ—** (е‘јгҒіеҮәгҒ—ж·ұгҒ•з„Ўй–ўдҝӮ)гҖӮ setjmp гҒҢ 1 гӮ’иҝ”гҒ— (еҲқеӣһ 0)гҖҒ main гҒҢ for(EVER) гӮ’еҶҚйҖІе…ҘгҖҒ е…Ё getty гӮ’ kill гҒ—гҒҰ /etc/ttys еҶҚиӘӯиҫјгҖӮ гҒ“гӮҢгҒҢ **гҖҢ**SIGHUP = иЁӯе®ҡгғӘгғӯгғјгғү**гҖҚ ж…Јзҝ’**гҒ®иө·жәҗвҖ”вҖ”sendmail/nginx/inetd/cron/syslogd/dnsmasq гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰгҒҢ SIGHUP гҒ§гғӘгғӯгғјгғүгҖӮ setjmp/longjmp гҒҜ C гҒ®е”ҜдёҖгҒ®зө„гҒҝиҫјгҒҝйқһгғӯгғјгӮ«гғ«и„ұеҮәвҖ”вҖ”дҫӢеӨ–гҒӘгҒ—гҖҒ й–ўж•°и·ЁгҒҺ goto гҒӘгҒ—гҖӮ reset() гҒҜ 1 иЎҢ (`longjmp(sjbuf, 1)`)вҖ”вҖ”гҖҢиЁӯе®ҡгғӘгғӯгғјгғүгҖҚ иЁӯиЁҲгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғіе…ЁдҪ“гҒҢгҒ“гҒ® 1 иЎҢгҒ«гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: getty cycles through baud rates via table chain. itab[] entries have `tname` (current) and `nname` (next-if-failed). When dialup-line is opened, getty tries the named entry (e.g. '0' = B300), prints login prompt, calls getname(). If user sends BREAK (NUL byte), getname returns 0, getty moves to nname (e.g. 1 = B1200), retries. Cycle: '0' вҶ’ 1 вҶ’ 2 вҶ’ 3 вҶ’ '0' covering 300, 1200, 150, 110 baud. Fixed-speed entries have nname == tname (no cycling for hardwired consoles). Login messages differ per speed: B300 has VT escape + bell, B110 is plain. Auto-baud detection without explicit speed handshake вҖ” the user's BREAK key communicates 'try slower'.
ai_analysis.human_story: 1979 dialup: you call the computer, modem answers, getty starts. But what speed is your terminal? 110? 300? 1200? Modems didn't auto-negotiate yet. getty's clever answer: try one speed, print `login:`, see what comes back. If user hits BREAK (their terminal's escape from the wrong speed), getty rotates to next speed and re-prints `login:`. Eventually one works. The login messages embed VT escape sequences and bell characters to help the user know something's there. Pre-Hayes-modem world: getty was the auto-baud detector itself.

appraiser_note: getty гҒҜ baud rate гӮ’гғҶгғјгғ–гғ«гғҒгӮ§гӮӨгғігҒ§е·Ўеӣһи©ҰиЎҢгҖӮ itab[] гҒ®еҗ„гӮЁгғігғҲгғӘгҒ« `tname` (зҸҫеңЁгҒ®гғҶгғјгғ–гғ«еҗҚ) гҒЁ `nname` (еӨұж•—жҷӮгҒ®ж¬Ў) гҒҢгҒӮгӮҠгҖҒ BREAK (NUL гғҗгӮӨгғҲ) гӮ’еҸ—гҒ‘еҸ–гҒЈгҒҹгӮү nname гҒёйҒ·з§»гҖӮ е·Ўеӣһ: `'0' вҶ’ 1 вҶ’ 2 вҶ’ 3 вҶ’ '0'` гҒ§ 300/1200/150/110 baud гӮ’иҰҶгҒҶгҖӮ еӣәе®ҡ speed гӮЁгғігғҲгғӘгҒҜ `nname == tname` (гӮігғігӮҪгғјгғ«зӯүгғҸгғјгғүгӮҰгӮ§гӮўеӣәе®ҡз”Ё)гҖӮ login гғЎгғғгӮ»гғјгӮёгҒҜ speed гҒ”гҒЁгҒ«еҫ®еҰҷгҒ«йҒ•гҒҶвҖ”вҖ”B300 гҒҜ VT escape + гғҷгғ« (\007)гҖҒ B110 гҒҜзҙ жңҙгҖӮ гғўгғҮгғ гҒҢ Auto-Negotiate гҒ—гҒӘгҒ„жҷӮд»Ј (Hayes д»ҘеүҚ) гҒ® auto-baud жӨңеҮәвҖ”вҖ”гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјгҒ® BREAK гӮӯгғјгҒҢгҖҢгӮӮгҒЈгҒЁйҒ…гҒ„йҖҹеәҰгӮ’и©ҰгҒӣгҖҚ гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶдҝЎеҸ·гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰж©ҹиғҪгҒ—гҒҹгҖӮ 1979 е№ҙгҒ®гғҖгӮӨгғӨгғ«гӮўгғғгғ—з’°еўғгҒ®зё®еӣігҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: V6/V7 Unix terminal control uses struct sgttyb: sg_ispeed, sg_ospeed, sg_erase, sg_kill, sg_flags. stty(0, &tmode) syscall sets terminal mode on fd 0. Flag bits: RAW, ANYP/EVENP/ODDP (parity), ECHO, CRMOD (CR вҶ’ CR/LF), LCASE (upper вҶ’ lower), XTABS (TAB вҶ’ spaces), NL1/CR1/CR2/FF1/TAB1 (character output delays вҖ” relics of mechanical teletypes needing physical time for carriage return). 4.2BSD (1983) introduced struct tcsetattr/tcgetattr and richer struct termios with c_iflag/c_oflag/c_cflag/c_lflag (4 flag words instead of 1). getty illustrates 2-stage configuration: iflags during getname() (RAW, no echo), then fflags after name confirmed (cooked, echo on).
ai_analysis.human_story: Before termios (1983), there was sgttyb. Five fields: input speed, output speed, erase char, kill char, flags. The flags packed RAW, ECHO, parity, case conversion, and even physical-delay-after-character into one int. The delay bits (NL1, CR1, CR2) were for mechanical teletypes вҖ” Carriage Return took real time, so after CR you needed to wait 1 or 2 character times before sending the next byte, or it would print mid-stroke. getty's two-stage config (iflags for prompt, fflags for after login) is the same pattern modern tty drivers use, just with finer-grained flags. The mechanical past lives in V7's terminal ioctl bits.

appraiser_note: V6/V7 Unix гҒ® **гӮҝгғјгғҹгғҠгғ«еҲ¶еҫЎ API**: `struct sgttyb` (sg_ispeed/sg_ospeed/sg_erase/sg_kill/sg_flags гҒ® 5 гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғү) гҒЁ `stty(0, &tmode)` syscallгҖӮ гғ•гғ©гӮ°гғ“гғғгғҲ: RAWгҖҒ ANYP/EVENP/ODDP (гғ‘гғӘгғҶгӮЈ)гҖҒ ECHOгҖҒ CRMODгҖҒ LCASEгҖҒ XTABSгҖҒ гҒқгҒ—гҒҰ NL1/CR1/CR2/FF1/TAB1 (еҗ„ж–Үеӯ—еҫҢгҒ®йҒ…е»¶)гҖӮ йҒ…е»¶гғ•гғ©гӮ°гҒҜ **ж©ҹжў°ејҸгғҶгғ¬гӮҝгӮӨгғ—**гҒ®еҗҚж®ӢвҖ”вҖ”гӮӯгғЈгғӘгғғгӮёгғӘгӮҝгғјгғігҒ«зү©зҗҶжҷӮй–“гҒҢгҒӢгҒӢгӮӢгҒ®гҒ§гҖҒ CR еҫҢ 1гҖң2 ж–Үеӯ—еҲҶеҫ…гҒҹгҒӘгҒ„гҒЁеҚ°еӯ—гҒҢиЎҢгҒ®йҖ”дёӯгҒ§е§ӢгҒҫгӮӢгҖӮ 4.2BSD (1983) гҒ§ `struct termios` (4 гҒӨгҒ®гғ•гғ©гӮ°гғҜгғјгғү c_iflag/c_oflag/c_cflag/c_lflag) гҒЁ tcsetattr/tcgetattr гҒ«зҪ®гҒҚжҸӣгҒҲгҖӮ getty гҒ® **2 ж®өйҡҺиЁӯе®ҡ** (iflags гҒ§ getname() дёӯгҒ® RAW гғўгғјгғүгҖҒ fflags гҒ§гғӯгӮ°гӮӨгғіеҫҢгҒ® cooked гғўгғјгғү) гҒҜзҸҫд»ЈгҒ® tty driver гҒ§гӮӮеҗҢгҒҳгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҖӮ ж©ҹжў°ејҸжҷӮд»ЈгҒ®йҒәз”ЈгҒҢ V7 ioctl гғ“гғғгғҲгҒ«еҲ»еҚ°гҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: RAW mode means kernel doesn't do line editing вҖ” getty does it manually. ERASE = '#' deletes previous char (substitute for BS), KILL = '@' clears entire line (substitute for line-clear). Why these? ASR-33 Teletype had no BS key; '#' and '@' are printable-but-rarely-needed characters chosen as in-band edit codes. Username characters classified: a-z increments lower counter, A-Z increments upper counter (and lowered into name buffer), space becomes underscore (since /etc/passwd uses ':' as field separator and spaces in names break parsing). After loop, if upper>0 && lower==0, terminal is all-caps (Teletype ASR-33 style) and LCASE flag gets set so subsequent kernel output translates lowercase вҶ’ uppercase. Username detection ALSO detects terminal capability вҖ” clever multi-purpose code.
ai_analysis.human_story: Modern terminals have a Backspace key. ASR-33 Teletype (1963) didn't вҖ” it had no way to erase. So Unix conventions: `#` deletes one char, `@` kills the whole line. These are visible characters, sent like any other, but interpreted by getty as edit commands. The all-caps username detection is even cleverer: if you type `BOB`, getty deduces 'this is a Teletype, output should be uppercase too' and tells the kernel via LCASE flag. One function reads the name AND fingerprints the terminal. Modern bash still inherits the # convention as the comment character partly because of this history.

appraiser_note: RAW гғўгғјгғүгҒ§гҒҜ kernel гҒҢиЎҢз·ЁйӣҶгӮ’гҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҒ®гҒ§гҖҒ getty гҒҢжүӢеӢ•е®ҹиЈ…гҖӮ **ERASE = '#'** гҒҢзӣҙеүҚ 1 ж–Үеӯ—ж¶ҲеҺ» (BS гҒ®д»Јжӣҝ)гҖҒ **KILL = '@'** гҒҢиЎҢе…ЁдҪ“гӮҜгғӘгӮўгҖӮ гҒӘгҒңгҒ“гӮҢгӮүпјҹ ASR-33 гғҶгғ¬гӮҝгӮӨгғ— (1963) гҒ«гҒҜ BS гӮӯгғјгҒҢз„ЎгҒҸгҖҒ еҚ°еӯ—еҸҜиғҪгҒ гҒҢж»…еӨҡгҒ«дҪҝгӮҸгҒӘгҒ„ж–Үеӯ—гӮ’гҖҢ**иЎҢеҶ…з·ЁйӣҶгӮігғјгғү**гҖҚ гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰйҒёгӮ“гҒ гҖӮ гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјеҗҚж–Үеӯ—гҒ®еҲҶйЎһ: a-z гҒ§ lower гӮ«гӮҰгғігӮҝгҖҒ A-Z гҒ§ upper гӮ«гӮҰгғігӮҝгӮ’еў—гӮ„гҒ—гҒӨгҒӨ name гғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎгҒ«гҒҜе°Ҹж–Үеӯ—еҢ–гҒ—гҒҰж јзҙҚгҖҒ гӮ№гғҡгғјгӮ№гҒҜ _ гҒ«зҪ®жҸӣ (/etc/passwd гҒ® : еҢәеҲҮгӮҠеҜҫзӯ–)гҖӮ гғ«гғјгғ—еҫҢ upper>0 && lower==0 гҒӘгӮүгғҶгғ¬гӮҝгӮӨгғ— ASR-33 йўЁз«Ҝжң«гҒЁеҲӨе®ҡгҖҒ LCASE гғ•гғ©гӮ°гӮ’иЁӯе®ҡгҒ—гҒҰд»ҘйҷҚгҒ® kernel еҮәеҠӣгҒ§е°Ҹж–Үеӯ—вҶ’еӨ§ж–Үеӯ—еӨүжҸӣгҖӮ **гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјеҗҚиӘӯгҒҝгҒЁз«Ҝжң«иғҪеҠӣжӨңеҮәгӮ’еҗҢжҷӮгҒ«иЎҢгҒҶ**вҖ”вҖ”1 й–ўж•°гҒ§еӨҡзӣ®зҡ„гҖҒ 1979 жөҒгҒ®зөҢжёҲиЁӯиЁҲгҖӮ зҸҫд»Ј bash гҒ® # гӮігғЎгғігғҲж–Үеӯ—ж…Јзҝ’гҒ«гӮӮгҒ“гҒ®жӯҙеҸІгҒҢж®ӢгӮӢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: partab[] precomputes even parity bit (bit 7) for each 7-bit ASCII character. To send char c: c |= partab[c & 0177] & 0200 вҖ” strip to 7 bits, lookup parity bit, OR it in. Why? Serial dialup lines flip bits due to phone-line noise; even parity (bit 7 set so total 1s count is even) catches all single-bit errors at receiver. Table lookup vs runtime calculation: 128 bytes of memory for O(1) parity instead of ~5-10 instructions of XOR-based bit counting. On PDP-11, the table approach was 2 instructions vs 10. The data table embedded in source (128 bytes octal) is V7-era style; modern compilers use intrinsics or hardware __builtin_parity().
ai_analysis.human_story: Dialup phone lines flip bits. Noise on the analog signal can turn a 1 into 0 or vice versa. 1979's solution: even parity. Set bit 7 so the total count of 1s in the byte is always even. Receiver checks вҖ” if it sees odd count, error. 100% detection of single-bit flips. getty's partab[] is the lookup table: 128 bytes, one per ASCII code, with bit 7 = parity. Sending char becomes: lookup, OR, write вҖ” 3 instructions. Modern x86 has popcnt and __builtin_parity intrinsics; PDP-11 didn't, hence the table. Fast error detection over noisy phone lines, in 128 bytes.

appraiser_note: partab[] гҒҜ **ASCII еҗ„ж–Үеӯ— (0гҖң127) гҒ®еҒ¶ж•°гғ‘гғӘгғҶгӮЈгғ“гғғгғҲ (bit 7) гӮ’дәӢеүҚиЁҲз®—**гҒ—гҒҹ 128 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгғҶгғјгғ–гғ«гҖӮ йҖҒдҝЎжҷӮ `c |= partab[c & 0177] & 0200` гҒ§дёӢдҪҚ 7 гғ“гғғгғҲгӮ’еҸ–гӮҠгҖҒ гғ‘гғӘгғҶгӮЈгғ“гғғгғҲгӮ’ ORгҖӮ гҒӘгҒңпјҹ гғҖгӮӨгғӨгғ«гӮўгғғгғ—йӣ»и©ұз·ҡгҒҜ **гғ“гғғгғҲеҸҚи»ўгғҺгӮӨгӮә**гҒҢиө·гҒҚгӮӢвҖ”вҖ”еҒ¶ж•°гғ‘гғӘгғҶгӮЈ (bit 7 гҒ§е…Ё 1 ж•°гӮ’еҒ¶ж•°гҒ«) гҒ§еҸ—дҝЎеҒҙгҒҢ **1 гғ“гғғгғҲгӮЁгғ©гғјгӮ’ 100% жӨңеҮә**гҒ§гҒҚгӮӢгҖӮ гғҶгғјгғ–гғ«ж–№ејҸ vs гғ©гғігӮҝгӮӨгғ иЁҲз®—: 128 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгғЎгғўгғӘгҒ§ O(1) гғ‘гғӘгғҶгӮЈгҖҒ гғ©гғігӮҝгӮӨгғ гҒӘгӮү XOR гғҷгғјгӮ№гҒ§ 5гҖң10 е‘Ҫд»ӨгҖӮ PDP-11 гҒ§гҒҜ table lookup гҒҢ 2 е‘Ҫд»ӨгҖҒ XOR гғҷгғјгӮ№гҒҢ 10 е‘Ҫд»ӨвҖ”вҖ”йҖҹеәҰе·®гҒҢжұәе®ҡзҡ„гҖӮ 8 йҖІиЁҳиҝ° (0201 = 0x81гҖҒ bit 7 + bit 0) гҒҜ V7 жөҒгҖӮ зҸҫд»Ј x86 гҒ® popcnt гӮ„ __builtin_parity intrinsic гҒ®зҘ–гҖҒ гғҺгӮӨгӮёгғјгҒӘйӣ»и©ұз·ҡгӮ’ 128 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгҒ§е®ҲгҒЈгҒҹгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Unix authentication core in 5 lines. getpass('Password:') reads input with echo off (tty raw mode toggle inside the function). crypt(input, salt) uses modified-DES 25-round one-way hash with 12-bit salt (2 chars). Trick: pass pwd->pw_passwd as 2nd arg вҖ” crypt extracts the first 2 chars as salt, so we don't need to track salt separately. Compare via strcmp. /etc/passwd never stores plaintext; even file disclosure doesn't directly reveal passwords. The salt thwarts rainbow tables (4096x cost increase). 8-char password limit baked into crypt's design. *pw_passwd == '\0' empty-password check allows passwordless guest accounts.
ai_analysis.human_story: Five lines that define Unix authentication for 50 years. getpass turns off terminal echo, reads what you type without showing it, restores echo, returns the string. crypt slow-hashes the password with a 12-bit salt. /etc/passwd holds only hashes вҖ” file leak doesn't directly betray passwords. Salt frustrates rainbow tables by 4096x. 25 DES rounds was 'slow enough' in 1979; today it's trivially brute-forced. Modern systems use bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 in /etc/shadow. But the pattern вҖ” getpass + slow-hash + compare вҖ” survived. Every login prompt in SSH, su, sudo descends from these 5 lines.

appraiser_note: Unix иӘҚиЁјгҒ®ж ёеҝғгҖҒ гӮҸгҒҡгҒӢ 5 иЎҢгҖӮ `getpass("Password:")` гҒҢгӮЁгӮігғјз„ЎгҒ—гҒ§е…ҘеҠӣгӮ’иӘӯгҒҝ (й–ўж•°еҶ…гҒ§ tty raw гғўгғјгғүеҲҮжӣҝ)гҖҒ `crypt(input, salt)` гҒҢж”№йҖ  DES 25 гғ©гӮҰгғігғүгҒ®дёҖж–№еҗ‘гғҸгғғгӮ·гғҘ + 12 bit salt (2 ж–Үеӯ—)гҖӮ е·ҘеӨ«: з¬¬ 2 еј•ж•°гҒ« `pwd->pw_passwd` гӮ’жёЎгҒҷвҖ”вҖ”crypt гҒҜе…Ҳй ӯ 2 ж–Үеӯ—гӮ’ salt гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰжҠҪеҮәгҒҷгӮӢгҒ®гҒ§гҖҒ еҲҘйҖ” salt гӮ’з®ЎзҗҶгҒҷгӮӢеҝ…иҰҒгҒҢгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ strcmp гҒ§жҜ”ијғгҖӮ /etc/passwd гҒ«гҒҜ **гғҸгғғгӮ·гғҘгҒ®гҒҝ**дҝқеӯҳгҖҒ гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«жјҸжҙ©гҒ§гӮӮзӣҙжҺҘгғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүгҒҜжјҸгӮҢгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ salt гҒҜ rainbow table ж”»ж’ғгӮ’ 4096 еҖҚгӮігӮ№гғҲгҒ«гҖӮ 8 ж–Үеӯ—гғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүеҲ¶йҷҗгҒҜ crypt иЁӯиЁҲдёҠгҒ®гӮӮгҒ®гҖӮ `*pw_passwd == '\0'` з©әгғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүгғҒгӮ§гғғгӮҜгҒ§гӮІгӮ№гғҲгӮўгӮ«гӮҰгғігғҲгӮ’иЁұгҒҷгҖӮ SSHгҖҒ suгҖҒ sudo гҒ®иӘҚиЁјгғ—гғӯгғігғ—гғҲгҒҷгҒ№гҒҰгҒ®зҘ–гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Defense against username enumeration: if getpwnam() returns NULL (user doesn't exist), substitute a fake struct passwd with pw_passwd='nope' so the password prompt still appears, crypt() still runs, and the comparison still fails. Same timing, same UI, same error message ('Login incorrect') for both 'wrong password' and 'user doesn't exist'. Attacker can't probe usernames by observing whether the password prompt appears or how long it takes. The 'nope' string isn't a valid crypt output (not 13 chars), so strcmp always fails. This is one of the earliest documented timing-attack defenses in Unix вҖ” 1979 programmers already understood that login responses leak information.
ai_analysis.human_story: What if you mistype your username? login could say 'No such user' immediately. Or it could pretend the user exists, ask for a password, and reject. The 1979 login chose the second вҖ” defense against username enumeration. Type any name, login asks for password, login rejects. Attacker can't tell which usernames exist. The trick: a struct passwd called `nouser` with pw_passwd='nope' substituted when getpwnam returns NULL. crypt runs on user input, strcmp fails. Same code path, same time taken, same error message. Modern PAM and shadow do this exactly the same way. The username enumeration concept is 1990s+; the defense is 1979.

appraiser_note: гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјеҗҚеҲ—жҢҷж”»ж’ғгҒёгҒ®йҳІиЎӣ: getpwnam() гҒҢ NULL гӮ’иҝ”гҒҷ (гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјдёҚеӯҳеңЁ) жҷӮгҖҒ еҒҪгҒ® struct passwd `nouser = {"", "nope"}` гӮ’д»Је…ҘгҖӮ гҒ“гӮҢгҒ§гғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүгғ—гғӯгғігғ—гғҲгҒҢеҮәгӮӢгҖҒ crypt() гҒҢе®ҹиЎҢгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖҒ жҜ”ијғгҒҢеӨұж•—гҒҷгӮӢвҖ”вҖ”жң¬зү©гҒ®гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјгҒ§й–“йҒ•гҒЈгҒҹгғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүгӮ’е…ҘгӮҢгҒҹжҷӮгҒЁ **еҗҢгҒҳгӮҝгӮӨгғҹгғігӮ°гҖҒ еҗҢгҒҳ UIгҖҒ еҗҢгҒҳгӮЁгғ©гғјгғЎгғғгӮ»гғјгӮёгҖҢLogin incorrectгҖҚ**гҖӮ ж”»ж’ғиҖ…гҒҜгғ‘гӮ№гғҜгғјгғүгғ—гғӯгғігғ—гғҲгҒ®жңүз„ЎгӮ„еҝңзӯ”жҷӮй–“гҒӢгӮүгғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјеҗҚгӮ’еүІгӮҠеҮәгҒӣгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ `"nope"` гҒҜжңүеҠ№гҒӘ crypt еҮәеҠӣгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„ (13 ж–Үеӯ—гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„) гҒ®гҒ§ strcmp гҒҜеҝ…гҒҡеӨұж•—гҖӮ Unix гҒ§**жңҖгӮӮж—©жңҹгҒ«ж–ҮжӣёеҢ–гҒ•гӮҢгҒҹгӮҝгӮӨгғҹгғігӮ°ж”»ж’ғеҜҫзӯ–**гҒ® 1 гҒӨвҖ”вҖ”1979 е№ҙгҒ®гғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғһгҒҢгғӯгӮ°гӮӨгғіеҝңзӯ”гҒҢжғ…е ұгӮ’жјҸгӮүгҒҷгҒЁж—ўгҒ«зҗҶи§ЈгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒҹгҖӮ гҖҢгғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјеҗҚеҲ—жҢҷгҖҚ жҰӮеҝөгҒҜ 1990 е№ҙд»ЈгҒ гҒҢгҖҒ йҳІеҫЎгҒҜ 1979 е№ҙгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Privilege descent in 3 syscalls. login was forked from init with uid=0 (root). After authentication: chown(ttyn, uid, gid) transfers tty ownership to the new user (must run as root). setgid(pwd->pw_gid) changes group first вҖ” must precede setuid because once you drop root, you lose the privilege to change gids. setuid(pwd->pw_uid) drops root irreversibly. Order is absolute: chown вҶ’ setgid вҶ’ setuid. Environment also sanitized: `environ = envinit` replaces inherited env with safe {HOME, PATH} only вҖ” prevents env-var-based attacks (LD_PRELOAD, malicious PATH). The empty pw_shell defaults to /bin/sh. This 6-line sequence is the principle-of-least-privilege made executable.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does Unix make sure you can't be root after logging in as yourself? Three syscalls in order: chown the terminal so the new user owns it, setgid to change group (while still root), setuid to become the user (and lose root forever вҖ” no syscall to climb back up). Environment also wiped to {HOME, PATH} only вҖ” no inherited LD_PRELOAD tricks, no malicious PATH. Six lines is all it takes to transition from 'most powerful process on the system' to 'an ordinary user'. The 1979 sequence is identical to what login does today on Linux. setuid вҶ’ no going back, by design.

appraiser_note: 3 гҒӨгҒ®гӮ·гӮ№гғҶгғ гӮігғјгғ«гҒ§жЁ©йҷҗйҷҚдёӢгҖӮ init гҒӢгӮү fork гҒ•гӮҢгҒҰ uid=0 (root) гҒ§иө·еӢ•гҒ—гҒҹ login гҒҢгҖҒ иӘҚиЁјжҲҗеҠҹеҫҢ: `chown(ttyn, uid, gid)` гҒ§ tty гӮ’ж–°гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјжүҖжңүгҒ« (root жЁ©йҷҗеҝ…иҰҒ)гҖҒ `setgid(gid)` гҒ§ group е…Ҳ (uid гӮ’жҚЁгҒҰгӮӢгҒЁ gid еӨүжӣҙжЁ©йҷҗгӮӮеӨұгҒҶгҒ®гҒ§)гҖҒ `setuid(uid)` гҒ§ **root гӮ’дёҚеҸҜйҖҶгҒ«еӨұгҒҶ**гҖӮ й ҶеәҸгҒҜзө¶еҜҫ: chown вҶ’ setgid вҶ’ setuidгҖӮ з’°еўғгӮӮжө„еҢ–: `environ = envinit` гҒ§з¶ҷжүҝз’°еўғгӮ’жҚЁгҒҰгҖҒ {HOME, PATH} гҒ®гҒҝгҒ®е®үе…ЁгҒӘй…ҚеҲ—гҒ«зҪ®жҸӣвҖ”вҖ”LD_PRELOAD ж”»ж’ғгӮ„жӮӘж„ҸгҒӮгӮӢ PATH ж”»ж’ғгӮ’йҳІжӯўгҖӮ з©ә pw_shell гҒҜ `/bin/sh` гғҮгғ•гӮ©гғ«гғҲгҖӮ 6 иЎҢгҒ§гҖҢжңҖеј·гғ—гғӯгӮ»гӮ№гҖҚ гҒӢгӮүгҖҢдёҖиҲ¬гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјгҖҚ гҒёгҒ®йҒ·з§»гҖҒ **жңҖе°ҸжЁ©йҷҗгҒ®еҺҹеүҮ**гӮ’е®ҹиЎҢеҸҜиғҪгҒӘеҪўгҒ§иЎЁзҸҫгҖӮ 50 е№ҙеҫҢгҒ® Linux login гӮӮеҗҢгҒҳгӮ·гғјгӮұгғігӮ№гҖҒ setuid гҒ«гҖҢжҲ»гӮӢйҒ“гҖҚ гҒҜиЁӯиЁҲдёҠеӯҳеңЁгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: login signals 'this is a login shell' to /bin/sh by setting argv[0] = '-sh' instead of 'sh'. Construction: minusnam[16] = '-' literal; rindex(/bin/sh, '/') finds last slash, namep++ skips past it to 'sh'; strcat(minusnam, 'sh') gives '-sh'. Then execlp(pwd->pw_shell, minusnam, 0) вҖ” pwd->pw_shell is the real executable path ('/bin/sh'), but argv[0] is '-sh'. The shell at startup checks argv[0][0] == '-' to identify itself as a login shell, then reads /etc/profile and ~/.profile. All major shells (sh/bash/csh/zsh/ksh/tcsh) implement this convention. `bash -l` is the modern explicit alternative. exec failure falls through to 'No shell' + exit.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why does `echo $0` in your terminal sometimes show `-bash` instead of `bash`? Because login.c started bash with argv[0] = '-bash' as a signal: 'this is a LOGIN shell, please source /etc/profile and ~/.bash_profile'. The 1979 trick: stick '-' in front of the basename of the shell, pass it as argv[0]. Every modern shell honors this. bash -l is explicit; -bash is implicit. The minusnam[16] = '-' literal is a tiny piece of code with 50 years of consequences. Inheritance protocols don't need to be elegant to last; they just need to work.

appraiser_note: login гҒҜ **`argv[0] = "-sh"` гҒ§ /bin/sh гҒ«гҖҢгҒ“гӮҢгҒҜгғӯгӮ°гӮӨгғігӮ·гӮ§гғ«гҖҚ гҒЁдјқгҒҲгӮӢ**гҖӮ ж§ӢзҜү: `minusnam[16] = "-"` гғӘгғҶгғ©гғ«гҖҒ `rindex(/bin/sh, '/')` гҒ§жңҖеҫҢгҒ® `/` гӮ’иҰӢгҒӨгҒ‘гҖҒ `namep++` гҒ§ `sh` гҒ®е…Ҳй ӯгҒёгҖҒ `strcat(minusnam, "sh")` гҒ§ `"-sh"` е®ҢжҲҗгҖӮ `execlp(pwd->pw_shell, minusnam, 0)` гҒ§е®ҹдҪ“гҒҜ /bin/sh гҒ гҒҢ argv[0] гҒҜ -shгҖӮ shell иө·еӢ•жҷӮгҒ« `argv[0][0] == '-'` гҒ§иҮӘиә«гӮ’ **login shell** гҒЁеҲӨе®ҡгҖҒ /etc/profile гҒЁ ~/.profile гӮ’иӘӯгӮҖгҖӮ е…ЁгӮ·гӮ§гғ« (sh/bash/csh/zsh/ksh/tcsh) гҒҢгҒ“гҒ®ж…Јзҝ’гӮ’е®ҹиЈ…гҖӮ `bash -l` гҒҜзҸҫд»ЈгҒ®жҳҺзӨәзүҲгҖӮ exec еӨұж•—жҷӮгҒҜ "No shell" + exitгҖӮ `echo $0` гҒ§ `-bash` гҒЁеҮәгӮӢгҒ®гҒҜ login.c гҒҢгҒ“гҒ®иҰҸзҙ„гӮ’ 1979 е№ҙгҒ«зўәз«ӢгҒ—гҒҹгҒӢгӮүвҖ”вҖ”50 е№ҙгҒ®з¶ҷжүҝгӮ’жҢҒгҒӨ tiny piece of codeгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: compile() translates regex string to bytecode array (expbuf). 8 opcodes: CCHR (char literal), CDOT (.), CCL (char class), NCCL (negated cc), CDOL ($), CEOF (end), CBRA (\(), CKET (\)), CBACK (\1-\9). STAR (bit 01) is OR'd onto previous opcode: `a*` becomes 2 bytes: (CCHR | STAR), 'a'. The bit-overlay design saves memory вҖ” no separate STAR opcode needed. `^` sets circf flag for line-anchored match. The compileвҶ’execute split is now standard for regex engines (Python re.compile, JavaScript RegExp constructor, Rust regex::Regex::new), but in 1979 it was novel. compile is one-pass linear; produces expbuf which becomes input to advance().
ai_analysis.human_story: Ken Thompson's regex notation needed an internal form. compile() solves it: walk the pattern, emit opcodes. The STAR-as-OR-bit trick is elegance born of memory scarcity: `a*` becomes 2 bytes, not 3. Modern regex engines compile to richer NFAs or DFAs, but the compileвҶ’execute split is universal. Type `re.compile(r'a*')` in Python вҖ” you're doing in 2026 what Thompson formalized for ed's regex in the early 1970s and grep inherited in 1973.

appraiser_note: compile() гҒҜжӯЈиҰҸиЎЁзҸҫж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮ’гғҗгӮӨгғҲгӮігғјгғүй…ҚеҲ— (expbuf) гҒ«еӨүжҸӣгҖӮ 8 гӮӘгғҡгӮігғјгғү: CCHR (ж–Үеӯ—е®ҡж•°)гҖҒ CDOT (.)гҖҒ CCL (ж–Үеӯ—гӮҜгғ©гӮ№)гҖҒ NCCL (еҗҰе®ҡ CC)гҖҒ CDOL ($)гҖҒ CEOF (зөӮз«Ҝ)гҖҒ CBRA (\()гҖҒ CKET (\))гҖҒ CBACK (\1-\9)гҖӮ **STAR (bit 01) гҒҜзӣҙеүҚ opcode гҒ« OR**: `a*` гҒҜ 2 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ `(CCHR | STAR), 'a'`гҖӮ гғ“гғғгғҲйҮҚгҒӯиЁӯиЁҲгҒ§зңҒгғЎгғўгғӘвҖ”вҖ”STAR е°Ӯз”Ё opcode гҒҢдёҚиҰҒгҖӮ `^` гҒҜ circf гғ•гғ©гӮ°гҒ§иЎҢй ӯгӮўгғігӮ«гғјгҖӮ compileвҶ’execute еҲҶйӣўгҒҜзҸҫд»ЈгҒ® regex гӮЁгғігӮёгғі (Python re.compileгҖҒ JavaScript RegExpгҖҒ Rust regex::Regex::new) гҒ®жЁҷжә–гҒ гҒҢгҖҒ 1979 е№ҙгҒ§гҒҜ novelгҖӮ ed гҒ® Thompson regex гӮ’ grep гҒҢ 1973 е№ҙгҒ«зӢ¬з«ӢеҢ–гҖҒ гҒқгҒ®гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ«жҲҰз•ҘгҒҢгҒ“гҒ“гҒ«гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: advance() is a bytecode VM. CCHR/CDOT/CDOL/CEOF do immediate one-char matches and continue or return. CCHR|STAR implements greedy-then-backtrack: while loop consumes all matching chars (lp advances forward), then star: label uses do-while loop calling advance() recursively, decrementing lp on each failure вҖ” this is greedy matching with backtracking. For `a*b` on input `aaab`: first consume all `a`s (lp = end of aaa), then try matching `b` at each position from rightmost backward until success or curlp reached. The inner fast path (if(*ep == CCHR)) skips positions where the next required char doesn't match, avoiding wasteful recursive advance() calls. This bytecode VM + greedy + backtrack pattern is the ancestor of every regex engine: sed, awk, Perl, Python re, Ruby Regexp, Java Pattern, JavaScript RegExp.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why is regex matching so slow on pathological inputs like /a*a*a*a*b/? Because of this: greedy + backtrack. advance() consumes as much as possible (the greedy part), then recursively tries the rest. If it fails, lp-- (give back one char) and retry. Worst case, exponential time. The 1979 design is mathematically correct and easy to understand; modern engines (RE2, Rust regex) avoid backtracking entirely by compiling to DFAs, achieving linear time at the cost of less expressive regex (no backreferences). But every Perl-compatible regex engine вҖ” PCRE, JavaScript, Python вҖ” still uses Thompson's greedy+backtrack from 1973's grep.

appraiser_note: advance() гҒҜгғҗгӮӨгғҲгӮігғјгғү VMгҖӮ CCHR/CDOT/CDOL/CEOF гҒҜеҚіжҷӮ 1 ж–Үеӯ—гғһгғғгғҒгҖӮ **CCHR|STAR гҒҢ greedy + backtrack**: while гғ«гғјгғ—гҒ§дёҖиҮҙгҒҷгӮӢйҷҗгӮҠж¶ҲиІ» (lp еүҚйҖІ)гҖҒ star: гғ©гғҷгғ«гҒ§ж®ӢгӮҠгӮ’ advance() еҶҚеё°е‘јгҒіеҮәгҒ—гҖҒ еӨұж•—гҒӘгӮү lp-- гҒ§ 1 ж–Үеӯ—жҲ»гҒ—гҒҰеҶҚи©ҰиЎҢгҖӮ `a*b` гҒ§е…ҘеҠӣ `aaab` гҒ®е ҙеҗҲ: гҒҫгҒҡе…Ё `a` ж¶ҲиІ» (lp = aaa гҒ®ж¬Ў)гҖҒ гҒқгҒ“гҒӢгӮүеҫҢгӮҚеҗ‘гҒҚгҒ« `b` гғһгғғгғҒгӮ’и©ҰгҒ—гҖҒ жҲҗеҠҹгҒҫгҒҹгҒҜ curlp еҲ°йҒ”гҒҫгҒ§гҖӮ еҶ…еҒҙгҒ® fast path (`if(*ep == CCHR)`) гҒҜж¬ЎгҒ®еҝ…й Ҳж–Үеӯ—гҒҢдёҖиҮҙгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„дҪҚзҪ®гӮ’гӮ№гӮӯгғғгғ—гҖҒ з„Ўй§„гҒӘ advance() еҶҚеё°гӮ’еӣһйҒҝгҖӮ bytecode VM + greedy + backtrack гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒҢе…Ё regex гӮЁгғігӮёгғігҒ®зҘ–: sedгҖҒ awkгҖҒ PerlгҖҒ Python reгҖҒ RubyгҖҒ Java PatternгҖҒ JavaScript RegExpгҖӮ RE2 гӮ„ Rust regex гҒҜ backtrack гӮ’е®Ңе…ЁгҒ«йҒҝгҒ‘гҒҰ DFA гҒ§з·ҡеҪўжҷӮй–“гӮ’йҒ”жҲҗгҒҷгӮӢгҒҢгҖҒ PCRE / JS / Python гҒҜд»ҠгӮӮ Thompson гҒ® 1973 е№ҙж–№ејҸгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Char class `[abc]` or `[a-z]` stored as 16-byte bitmap (128 bits, sufficient for 7-bit ASCII). bittab[8] = {1,2,4,8,16,32,64,128} maps bit-index to bit-mask. To set bit for char c: `ep[c >> 3] |= bittab[c & 07]` вҖ” c >> 3 = byte index, c & 07 = bit position. Range `a-z` iterates from sp[-2] to *sp setting all bits in between. Negation `[^...]` flips all 16 bytes via XOR with -1, then clears bit 0 (NUL avoidance). Match in advance: `ep[c >> 3] & bittab[c & 07]` вҖ” O(1) lookup, no string scan. Modern regex engines extend this to 128KB bitmap for Unicode BMP, but the bit-twiddling logic is identical.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you test 'is c in [a-z]?' in O(1)? Build a bitmap: 128 bits where bit c is 1 if c is in the class. Test = single bit lookup. 1979's grep does this in 16 bytes. Range syntax 'a-z' becomes a for-loop setting 26 bits. Negation 'XOR with -1' flips them all in 16 instructions. The math is high-school combinatorics; the elegance is making it 16 bytes per char class instead of hundreds. Modern PCRE uses 8KB bitmaps for Unicode BMP; the principle is the same as 1979 grep вҖ” bit-tested set membership for character classes.

appraiser_note: ж–Үеӯ—гӮҜгғ©гӮ№ `[abc]` гӮ„ `[a-z]` гӮ’ **16 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ = 128 гғ“гғғгғҲгҒ®гғ“гғғгғҲгғһгғғгғ—**гҒ§ж јзҙҚ (7 bit ASCII гҒ§еҚҒеҲҶ)гҖӮ `bittab[8] = {1,2,4,8,16,32,64,128}` гҒҜгғ“гғғгғҲдҪҚзҪ®гҒӢгӮүгғһгӮ№гӮҜгҒёгҒ®еӨүжҸӣгғҶгғјгғ–гғ«гҖӮ ж–Үеӯ— c гҒ®гғ“гғғгғҲгӮ’з«ӢгҒҰгӮӢ: `ep[c >> 3] |= bittab[c & 07]`вҖ”вҖ”c >> 3 = гғҗгӮӨгғҲгӮӨгғігғҮгғғгӮҜгӮ№гҖҒ c & 07 = гғ“гғғгғҲдҪҚзҪ®гҖӮ зҜ„еӣІ `a-z` гҒҜ sp[-2] гҒӢгӮү *sp гҒҫгҒ§гғ«гғјгғ—гҒ§е…Ёгғ“гғғгғҲгӮ»гғғгғҲгҖӮ еҗҰе®ҡ `[^...]` гҒҜ 16 гғҗгӮӨгғҲе…ЁйғЁгӮ’ XOR -1 гҒ§еҸҚи»ўгҖҒ bit 0 (NUL) гҒ гҒ‘гӮҜгғӘгӮўгҖӮ advance гҒ§гҒ®гғһгғғгғҒгҒҜ `ep[c >> 3] & bittab[c & 07]`вҖ”вҖ”**O(1) дёҖзҷәжӨңжҹ»**гҖҒ ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮ№гӮӯгғЈгғідёҚиҰҒгҖӮ зҸҫд»ЈгҒ® PCRE гҒҜ Unicode BMP з”ЁгҒ« 8KB гғ“гғғгғҲгғһгғғгғ—гҒ«жӢЎејөгҒҷгӮӢгҒҢгҖҒ гғ“гғғгғҲж“ҚдҪңгғӯгӮёгғғгӮҜгҒҜ 1979 grep гҒЁеҗҢдёҖгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Fast path optimization: when pattern starts with literal char (opcode CCHR), only test positions where that char appears. The do-while loop with `if (*p1 != c) continue` skips non-matching positions and only calls advance() at matching ones. For pattern 'error' on 1000-line file with 10 occurrences of 'e', advance() is called 10 times instead of (avg-line-length Г— 1000) times. This is the proto-Boyer-Moore: scan for the first byte, do full match only at candidates. Modern grep variants (GNU grep, ag, ripgrep) implement Boyer-Moore-Horspool or Aho-Corasick with SIMD-accelerated byte scanning. The 1979 fast path is the ancestor.
ai_analysis.human_story: Naive regex matching tries advance() at every input position. Slow. grep's 1979 optimization: if pattern starts with literal 'e', only try advance() at positions where 'e' appears. This is the proto-Boyer-Moore вҖ” a poor man's version, decades before BM/BMH/Aho-Corasick became standard. ripgrep (2016) does the same thing with SIMD instructions scanning 32 bytes at a time. The fundamental insight вҖ” 'don't call the expensive matcher at positions that can't possibly match' вҖ” is from grep's 6-line fast path in 1979.

appraiser_note: fast path жңҖйҒ©еҢ–: гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒҢ**гғӘгғҶгғ©гғ«ж–Үеӯ—гҒ§е§ӢгҒҫгӮӢ** (opcode CCHR) е ҙеҗҲгҖҒ гҒқгҒ®ж–Үеӯ—гҒҢеҮәзҸҫгҒҷгӮӢдҪҚзҪ®гҒ§гҒ®гҒҝ advance() гӮ’е‘јгҒ¶гҖӮ do-while гғ«гғјгғ—гҒ® `if (*p1 != c) continue` гҒ§йқһгғһгғғгғҒдҪҚзҪ®гӮ’гӮ№гӮӯгғғгғ—гҖҒ гғһгғғгғҒдҪҚзҪ®гҒ§гҒ®гҒҝ advance()гҖӮ гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғі 'error' гҒ§ 1000 иЎҢгғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«дёӯ 'e' гҒҢ 10 з®ҮжүҖгҒӘгӮүгҖҒ advance() е‘јгҒіеҮәгҒ—гҒҜ 10 еӣһ (vs е№іеқҮиЎҢй•· Г— 1000 еӣһ)гҖӮ гҒ“гӮҢгҒҢ **proto-Boyer-Moore**вҖ”вҖ”е…Ҳй ӯгғҗгӮӨгғҲгҒ§гӮ№гӮӯгғЈгғігҖҒ еҖҷиЈңдҪҚзҪ®гҒ§гҒ®гҒҝгғ•гғ«гғһгғғгғҒгҖӮ зҸҫд»ЈгҒ® GNU grepгҖҒ agгҖҒ ripgrep гҒҜ Boyer-Moore-Horspool гӮ„ Aho-Corasick + SIMD гғҗгӮӨгғҲгӮ№гӮӯгғЈгғігҒ§е®ҹиЈ…гҖҒ ripgrep гҒҜ 32 гғҗгӮӨгғҲдёҰеҲ—гӮ№гӮӯгғЈгғігҖӮ гҖҢ**гғһгғғгғҒгҒ—гҒҲгҒӘгҒ„дҪҚзҪ®гҒ§й«ҳгӮігӮ№гғҲгғһгғғгғҒгғЈгӮ’е‘јгҒ°гҒӘгҒ„**гҖҚ гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶж №жң¬зҡ„жҙһеҜҹгҒҢгҖҒ 6 иЎҢгҒ® fast path гҒ«гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: grep -y (case-insensitive) is implemented by expanding the pattern itself: each lowercase letter becomes [Uu] char class before compile() runs. Pattern `abc` вҶ’ `[Aa][Bb][Cc]`. Regex engine itself stays unchanged вҖ” char class machinery handles both cases. Existing `[...]` sequences pass through unmodified (the while loop), backslash escapes pass through too. Limitations: uppercase input letters don't expand (only islower triggers), and existing [abc] don't get expanded to [aAbBcC]. Later -i flag (4.0BSD) fixed this by adding case-insensitive logic inside the matcher. But the 1979 design вҖ” feature = pattern transformation, not engine extension вҖ” is the Unix philosophy in 17 lines.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you add case-insensitive matching without changing the regex engine? Expand the pattern. abc becomes [Aa][Bb][Cc]. The regex engine, blissfully unchanged, sees a normal pattern with char classes. 17 lines added a feature without touching the 300+ lines of compile() and advance(). Limitations exist (uppercase doesn't expand, existing [abc] doesn't get extended), but the design is the Unix philosophy made concrete: features = composition of existing primitives, not extensions to the engine. The -i flag in 4.0BSD took the engine-modification approach instead вҖ” more flexible, more code.

appraiser_note: grep -y (case-insensitive) гҒҜ **гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғіиҮӘдҪ“гӮ’еұ•й–Ӣ**гҒ—гҒҰе®ҹиЈ…: еҗ„е°Ҹж–Үеӯ—гӮ’ [Uu] ж–Үеӯ—гӮҜгғ©гӮ№гҒ«еӨүжҸӣгҖҒ compile() еүҚж®өгҒ§гҖӮ гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғі `abc` вҶ’ `[Aa][Bb][Cc]`гҖӮ **regex гӮЁгғігӮёгғіжң¬дҪ“гҒҜеӨүжӣҙгӮјгғӯ**вҖ”вҖ”ж—ўеӯҳгҒ®ж–Үеӯ—гӮҜгғ©гӮ№ж©ҹиғҪгҒҢдёЎгӮұгғјгӮ№гӮ’еҮҰзҗҶгҖӮ ж—ўеӯҳ `[...]` гӮ·гғјгӮұгғігӮ№гҒҜгҒқгҒ®гҒҫгҒҫйҖҡйҒҺ (while гғ«гғјгғ—)гҖҒ гғҗгғғгӮҜгӮ№гғ©гғғгӮ·гғҘгӮЁгӮ№гӮұгғјгғ—гӮӮгҒқгҒ®гҒҫгҒҫгҖӮ еҲ¶йҷҗ: еӨ§ж–Үеӯ—е…ҘеҠӣгҒҜеұ•й–ӢгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„ (islower гҒ®гҒҝгғҲгғӘгӮ¬гғј)гҖҒ ж—ўеӯҳ [abc] гҒҜ [aAbBcC] гҒ«жӢЎејөгҒ•гӮҢгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ еҫҢгҒ® -i гғ•гғ©гӮ° (4.0BSD) гҒ§ matcher еҶ…йғЁгҒ« case-insensitive гғӯгӮёгғғгӮҜгӮ’иҝҪеҠ гҒ—гҒҰж”№е–„гҖӮ гҒ гҒҢ 1979 е№ҙгҒ®иЁӯиЁҲвҖ”вҖ”гҖҢ**ж©ҹиғҪ = гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғіеӨүжҸӣгҖҒ гӮЁгғігӮёгғіжӢЎејөгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸ**гҖҚвҖ”вҖ”гҒҜ **Unix е“ІеӯҰгӮ’ 17 иЎҢгҒ§е…·зҸҫеҢ–**гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: ed defines the Unix text editor command language in ~25 single-character commands: a/c/d/i (modify), p/P/l (display), g/v (global), s (substitute), q/Q (quit), r/w (file I/O), k (mark), m/t (move/copy), u (undo), ! (shell), = (address). Each command optionally takes [addr1[,addr2]] prefix. address() parses 6 forms: numeric, dot, dollar, +N/-N, /regex/, 'mark. This grammar (addresses + 1-char command + arguments) became the universal Unix editor language. vi's :%s/foo/bar/g, sed's 1,$d, grep's /pattern/ вҖ” all inherit. Written by Ken Thompson in 1971 (V1 Unix), 50 years before POSIX still mandates ed.
ai_analysis.human_story: Twenty-five characters of vocabulary, and you can edit any text file ever written. Ken Thompson invented this language in 1971 for the V1 Unix editor. The grammar was so good that vi (1976) adopted it as the colon-command mode. sed (1973) became 'ed without interaction'. grep (1973) became 'just the g/re/p part'. ex (vi's underlying engine) is ed with extensions. The 25 commands of ed are the vocabulary of Unix text processing вҖ” a one-letter language that defined how programmers talk to text for half a century.

appraiser_note: ed гҒҜ Unix гғҶгӮӯгӮ№гғҲгӮЁгғҮгӮЈгӮҝгӮігғһгғігғүиЁҖиӘһгӮ’ **зҙ„ 25 еҖӢгҒ® 1 ж–Үеӯ—гӮігғһгғігғү**гҒ§е®ҡзҫ©: a/c/d/i (з·ЁйӣҶ)гҖҒ p/P/l (иЎЁзӨә)гҖҒ g/v (global)гҖҒ s (substitute)гҖҒ q/Q (quit)гҖҒ r/w (file I/O)гҖҒ k (mark)гҖҒ m/t (move/copy)гҖҒ u (undo)гҖҒ ! (shell)гҖҒ = (address)гҖӮ еҗ„гӮігғһгғігғүгҒҜ optional `[addr1[,addr2]]` жҺҘй ӯиҫһгӮ’еҸ–гӮӢгҖӮ address() гҒҜ 6 еҪўејҸгӮ’гғ‘гғјгӮ№: ж•°еҖӨгҖҒ .гҖҒ $гҖҒ +N/-NгҖҒ /regex/гҖҒ 'markгҖӮ гҒ“гҒ®ж–Үжі• (гӮўгғүгғ¬гӮ№ + 1 ж–Үеӯ—гӮігғһгғігғү + еј•ж•°) гҒҢ **Unix гӮЁгғҮгӮЈгӮҝиЁҖиӘһ**гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰжҷ®йҒҚеҢ–вҖ”вҖ”vi гҒ® :%s/foo/bar/gгҖҒ sed гҒ® 1,$dгҖҒ grep гҒ® /pattern/гҖҒ гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰз¶ҷжүҝгҖӮ Ken Thompson гҒҢ 1971 е№ҙ (V1 Unix) гҒ«жӣёгҒҚгҖҒ 50 е№ҙеҫҢгҒ® POSIX гҒ§д»ҠгӮӮжЁҷжә–гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: ed.c and grep.c share the same regex opcode constants (CCHR=2, CDOT=4, CCL=6, NCCL=8, CDOL=10, CEOF=11, CKET=12, STAR=01, CBRA=1) вҖ” because grep was forked from ed's g/re/p command in 1973. The regex engine architecture is identical: compile() translates pattern to bytecode (expbuf), execute() runs a bytecode VM matching against text. Only CBACK differs: 14 in ed, 18 in grep (drifted during fork). The Thompson regex grammar вҖ” literal, ., *, [...], [^...], ^, $, \(...\), \1-\9 вҖ” became POSIX BRE. Every Unix regex tool (ed/sed/grep/awk/vi/vim) traces back to this engine.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why do ed, grep, sed, and vi all use the same regex syntax? Because they descended from the same code. Ken Thompson wrote the regex engine for ed (V1, 1971); grep (1973) was carved out as 'just g/re/p'; sed (1973) became 'non-interactive ed'; vi (1976) embedded ex which embedded ed. The opcode constants CCHR=2, CDOT=4, CCL=6 are identical across ed.c and grep.c because someone literally copy-pasted them. Half a century later, when you write `grep '^[a-z]*$'` you're typing exactly the syntax Thompson designed for ed in 1971.

appraiser_note: ed.c гҒЁ grep.c гҒҜ **еҗҢгҒҳ regex гӮӘгғҡгӮігғјгғүе®ҡж•°** (CCHR=2, CDOT=4, CCL=6, NCCL=8, CDOL=10, CEOF=11, CKET=12, STAR=01, CBRA=1) гӮ’е…ұжңүвҖ”вҖ”grep гҒҢ 1973 е№ҙгҒ« ed гҒ® g/re/p гӮігғһгғігғүгҒӢгӮү fork гҒ•гӮҢгҒҹгҒӢгӮүгҖӮ regex гӮЁгғігӮёгғіж§ӢйҖ гҒҜеҗҢдёҖ: compile() гҒҢгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігӮ’гғҗгӮӨгғҲгӮігғјгғү (expbuf) гҒ«зҝ»иЁігҖҒ execute() гҒҢгғҶгӮӯгӮ№гғҲгҒЁгғһгғғгғҒгҒҷгӮӢгғҗгӮӨгғҲгӮігғјгғү VMгҖӮ CBACK гҒ гҒ‘йҒ•гҒҶ (ed: 14гҖҒ grep: 18гҖҒ fork еҫҢгҒ«гғүгғӘгғ•гғҲ)гҖӮ Thompson regex ж–Үжі• (гғӘгғҶгғ©гғ«гҖҒ ., *, [...], [^...], ^, $, \(...\), \1-\9) гҒҢ **POSIX BRE** гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰжЁҷжә–еҢ–гҖӮ ed/sed/grep/awk/vi/vim гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰ 1971 е№ҙгҒ® Thompson гӮЁгғігӮёгғігҒ«йҒЎгӮӢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: ed stores the entire edited file in /tmp/eXXXXX as 256 blocks of 512 bytes (128KB max). Only two 512-byte buffers (ibuff, obuff) are in memory вҖ” a user-space implementation of OS swapping. tline encodes position as (block_no << 8) | (word_offset). zero[] array indexes line numbers вҶ’ tline pointers (one int per line = 2 bytes on PDP-11). 64K lines Г— 2 bytes = 128KB line index. When accessing different block: write old buffer to tempfile, read new one. Reading and writing have separate buffers (ibuff/obuff) to avoid thrashing when interleaving reads/writes. The 256-block limit was sufficient for V1 Unix source files. PDP-11 had ~256KB physical RAM total вҖ” ed's design used <2KB of buffer space.
ai_analysis.human_story: PDP-11 had 256KB total memory in 1971. Loading a 100KB file would consume 40% of RAM. Thompson's answer: don't load the file. Keep it in /tmp/eXXXXX as 256 blocks of 512 bytes (128KB max). Memory holds just two 512-byte buffers and a line index. Block access patterns work like OS paging: cache hit (same block) is free, cache miss writes old buffer and reads new. ed implemented swapping in user-space, 8 years before the 4.0BSD kernel got demand paging. The 'editor that doesn't fit the file in RAM' design pattern lived on in sed, awk, and grep вҖ” all stream-process files block by block.

appraiser_note: ed гҒҜ **з·ЁйӣҶдёӯгғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«е…ЁдҪ“гӮ’ /tmp/eXXXXX** гҒ« **256 гғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜ Г— 512 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ = 128KB** гҒ§дҝқжҢҒгҖӮ гғЎгғўгғӘгҒ«гҒҜ **гӮҸгҒҡгҒӢ 2 гҒӨгҒ® 512 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎ (ibuff/obuff)**вҖ”вҖ”гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјз©әй–“гҒ§гҒ® OS гӮ№гғҜгғғгғ”гғігӮ°е®ҹиЈ…гҖӮ tline гҒҜ (гғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜз•ӘеҸ· << 8) | гғҜгғјгғүгӮӘгғ•гӮ»гғғгғҲгҒ§гӮЁгғігӮігғјгғүгҖӮ zero[] й…ҚеҲ—гҒҢиЎҢз•ӘеҸ· вҶ’ tline гғқгӮӨгғігӮҝгҒ®зҙўеј• (1 иЎҢ = int = PDP-11 гҒ§ 2 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ)гҖҒ 64K иЎҢ Г— 2 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ = 128KB гҒ®иЎҢзҙўеј•гҖӮ йҒ•гҒҶгғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜгҒ«гӮўгӮҜгӮ»гӮ№гҒ—гҒҹгӮүеҸӨгҒ„гғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎгӮ’ tempfile гҒ«жӣёгҒҚжҲ»гҒ—гҖҒ ж–°гҒ—гҒ„гғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜгӮ’гғӯгғјгғүгҖӮ е…ҘеҠӣ/еҮәеҠӣгҒ§еҲҘгғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎ (ibuff/obuff) гӮ’жҢҒгҒӨгҒ®гҒҜ read/write дәӨдә’гҒ®гӮ№гғ©гғғгӮ·гғігӮ°йҳІжӯўгҖӮ PDP-11 256KB RAM жҷӮд»ЈгҖҒ ed гҒ®гғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎж¶ҲиІ»гҒҜ 2KB жңӘжәҖгҖӮ 4.0BSD гҒ®гғҮгғһгғігғүгғҡгғјгӮёгғігӮ°гӮҲгӮҠ 8 е№ҙж—©гҒ„гҖҒ гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјз©әй–“ swappingгҖӮ гҖҢгғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«гӮ’ RAM гҒ«д№—гҒӣгҒӘгҒ„гӮЁгғҮгӮЈгӮҝгҖҚ иЁӯиЁҲгҒҢ sed/awk/grep гҒ®гӮ№гғҲгғӘгғјгғ еҮҰзҗҶгҒёз¶ҷжүҝгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: global() implements g/regex/command вҖ” apply command to every matching line. Algorithm: (1) compile regex, (2) gather command string into globuf, (3) mark matching lines using bit 0 of line address (in-place flag), (4) for each marked line, set dot, redirect input to globuf, recursively call commands(). The 'globp' static enables nested input source switching. `v` is negated global (execute on non-matching lines). The literal command sequence `g/.../d` is so common it gets a dedicated gdelete() fast path (O(n) vs O(nВІ) for the general case where line numbers shift). The command name `grep` derives from `g/re/p` вҖ” this very function. sed's address language and global commands inherit directly.
ai_analysis.human_story: Type `g/error/d` in ed and every error-line vanishes. It's the killer feature: apply a command to all matching lines. global() does it via mark-and-execute: scan once to flag matches in the line address's bit 0, then loop executing the command on each flagged line. The recursion (commands() calls itself with input redirected to globuf) lets any ed command be the body. The special case for `g/.../d` is the speed hack вҖ” naive implementation is O(nВІ), the dedicated gdelete is O(n). The name `grep` came directly from this function: G-Regular Expression-Print, extracted into its own command in 1973.

appraiser_note: global() гҒҜ **g/regex/command** гӮ’е®ҹиЈ…вҖ”вҖ”гғһгғғгғҒгҒҷгӮӢе…ЁиЎҢгҒ«гӮігғһгғігғүйҒ©з”ЁгҖӮ гӮўгғ«гӮҙгғӘгӮәгғ : (1) regex гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ«гҖҒ (2) гӮігғһгғігғүж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮ’ globuf гҒ«и“„з©ҚгҖҒ (3) гғһгғғгғҒгҒҷгӮӢиЎҢгҒ« **иЎҢгӮўгғүгғ¬гӮ№гҒ® bit 0** гӮ’гғһгғјгӮҜ (in-place flag)гҖҒ (4) гғһгғјгӮҜгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹеҗ„иЎҢгҒ§ dot гӮ’иЁӯе®ҡгҖҒ commands() гҒ®е…ҘеҠӣгӮ’ globuf гҒ«гғӘгғҖгӮӨгғ¬гӮҜгғҲгҒ—гҒҰ **еҶҚеё°е‘јгҒіеҮәгҒ—**гҖӮ globp йқҷзҡ„еӨүж•°гҒ§е…ҘеҠӣгӮҪгғјгӮ№еҲҮжӣҝгӮ’е®ҹзҸҫгҖӮ `v` гҒҜ negated global (йқһгғһгғғгғҒиЎҢгҒ§е®ҹиЎҢ)гҖӮ `g/.../d` гҒҜйқһеёёгҒ«й »з№ҒгҒӘгҒ®гҒ§ **е°Ӯз”ЁгҒ® gdelete() й«ҳйҖҹгғ‘гӮ№** (жұҺз”Ё O(nВІ) vs е°Ӯз”Ё O(n))гҖӮ **гӮігғһгғігғүеҗҚ `grep` гҒ®иӘһжәҗ**вҖ”вҖ”g/re/pгҖҒ гҒ“гҒ®й–ўж•°гҒҢгҒқгҒ®гҒҫгҒҫ 1973 е№ҙгҒ«зӢ¬з«ӢгӮігғһгғігғүеҢ–гҖӮ sed гҒ®гӮўгғүгғ¬гӮ№иЁҖиӘһгҒЁ global гӮігғһгғігғүгҒҢзӣҙжҺҘз¶ҷжүҝгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: substitute() implements s/old/new/[flags]. Per line: execute regex, if match call dosub(); with g flag, loop until no more matches. dosub() builds genbuf: pre-match text + expanded replacement + post-match text. Replacement supports special chars: '&' = entire match, '\1'-'\9' = backreferences to \(...\) groups. This is the heart of sed вҖ” `sed s/old/new/` is exactly ed's substitute() running on each line of stdin. POSIX BRE inherits the & and \1-\9 syntax. vim's :s/.../.../, perl's s///, every regex-replace tool descends from this. The compile/execute regex pair plus substitute() forms the core text transformation engine of Unix.
ai_analysis.human_story: Type `s/foo/bar/` in ed вҖ” line becomes bar where foo was. Type `s/\(foo\)\(bar\)/\2\1/` вҖ” foobar becomes barfoo. Type `s/abc/&!/g` вҖ” every 'abc' becomes 'abc!'. These three operations вҖ” substitute, backref, '&' for match вҖ” are sed's entire raison d'ГӘtre, vim's :s command, perl's s/// operator. All extracted from ed's 70-line substitute() + dosub() pair. The '&' meaning 'whole match' and \1-\9 meaning 'capture groups' are 1971 conventions still binding in 2026. Five decades of regex-replace UI compressed into one Thompson algorithm.

appraiser_note: substitute() гҒҜ **s/old/new/[flags]** гӮ’е®ҹиЈ…гҖӮ иЎҢгҒ”гҒЁ: regex executeгҖҒ гғһгғғгғҒгҒ—гҒҹгӮү dosub()гҖҒ g гғ•гғ©гӮ°гҒӘгӮүжӣҙгҒ«гғһгғғгғҒгҒ—гҒӘгҒҸгҒӘгӮӢгҒҫгҒ§гғ«гғјгғ—гҖӮ dosub() гҒҜ genbuf гӮ’ж§ӢзҜү: гғһгғғгғҒеүҚ + еұ•й–ӢгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹзҪ®жҸӣж–Үеӯ—еҲ— + гғһгғғгғҒеҫҢгҖӮ зҪ®жҸӣж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гҒҜзү№ж®Ҡж–Үеӯ—гӮ’и§ЈйҮҲ: **`&` = гғһгғғгғҒе…ЁдҪ“**гҖҒ **`\1`гҖң`\9` = `\(...\)` гӮ°гғ«гғјгғ—гҒ®гғҗгғғгӮҜгғӘгғ•гӮЎгғ¬гғігӮ№**гҖӮ гҒ“гӮҢгҒҢ **sed гҒ®жң¬иіӘ**вҖ”вҖ”`sed s/old/new/` гҒҜ ed гҒ® substitute() гӮ’ stdin гҒ®еҗ„иЎҢгҒ§е®ҹиЎҢгҒҷгӮӢгҒ гҒ‘гҖӮ POSIX BRE гҒҢ `&` гҒЁ `\1`гҖң`\9` гӮ’з¶ҷжүҝгҖӮ vim гҒ® :s/...//гҖҒ perl гҒ® s///гҖҒ е…Ё regex зҪ®жҸӣгғ„гғјгғ«гҒҢзҘ–е…ҲгҖӮ compile/execute regex гғҡгӮў + substitute() гҒҢ **Unix гғҶгӮӯгӮ№гғҲеӨүжҸӣгӮЁгғігӮёгғігҒ®ж ёеҝғ**вҖ”вҖ”5 гғҡгӮўгҒ®ж“ҚдҪң (substituteгҖҒ backrefгҖҒ &) гҒҢ 50 е№ҙеҫҢгҒ® vim/sed/perl гҒ®зҪ®жҸӣ UI гӮ’ж”Ҝй…ҚгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: sed compiles its script into a fixed array ptrspace[100] of union reptr instructions. Each reptr holds: ad1/ad2 (addresses), re1 (regex), rhs (replacement), fcode (write file), command (1-byte opcode), and 4 flag bits (gfl/pfl/inar/negfl). reptr1 variant for normal commands, reptr2 variant for b/t branches (uses lb1 jump target instead of re1). The union shares the first 5 fields so the compiler can iterate the array without checking variant вҖ” common-prefix idiom. 25 opcodes: SCOM (substitute), DCOM (delete), GCOM (get hold), HCOM (hold), XCOM (exchange), BCOM (branch), TCOM (test-branch), YCOM (transliterate), ACOM (append), etc. PTRSIZE=100 means max 100 instructions per sed script вҖ” realistic in 1975.
ai_analysis.human_story: sed differs from ed in one critical way: it compiles. ed reads a command, executes, reads another. sed reads the whole script up-front, builds an instruction array, then runs each instruction on every input line. The union reptr is the compiled-instruction representation: address(es), regex, replacement, command code, flags. The reptr1/reptr2 union split is the C idiom for 'instructions look slightly different but share metadata' вҖ” same pattern modern VMs use for op encoding. PTRSIZE=100 limits scripts; today's GNU sed lifts this, but Lee McMahon's 1975 design assumed concise sed programs.

appraiser_note: sed гҒҜгӮ№гӮҜгғӘгғ—гғҲгӮ’ **еӣәе®ҡй…ҚеҲ— ptrspace[100]** гҒ® **union reptr е‘Ҫд»Ө**гҒ«гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ«гҒҷгӮӢгҖӮ еҗ„ reptr гҒҢ ad1/ad2 (гӮўгғүгғ¬гӮ№)гҖҒ re1 (regex)гҖҒ rhs (зҪ®жҸӣ)гҖҒ fcode (w гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«)гҖҒ command (1 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ opcode)гҖҒ 4 гғ•гғ©гӮ°гғ“гғғгғҲ (gfl/pfl/inar/negfl) гӮ’жҢҒгҒӨгҖӮ reptr1 гҒҢйҖҡеёёе‘Ҫд»ӨгҖҒ reptr2 гҒҢ b/t еҲҶеІҗ (re1 гҒ®д»ЈгӮҸгӮҠгҒ« lb1 jump target)гҖӮ union гҒҢе…Ҳй ӯ 5 гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғүгӮ’е…ұжңүгҒ—гҒҰгӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ©гҒҢ variant гӮ’ж„ҸиӯҳгҒӣгҒҡй…ҚеҲ—гӮ’иө°жҹ»гҒ§гҒҚгӮӢвҖ”вҖ”гҖҢе…ұйҖҡгғ—гғ¬гғ•гӮЈгғғгӮҜгӮ№гҖҚ гӮӨгғҮгӮЈгӮӘгғ гҖӮ 25 opcode: SCOM (substitute)гҖҒ DCOM (delete)гҖҒ GCOM (get hold)гҖҒ HCOM (hold)гҖҒ XCOM (exchange)гҖҒ BCOM (branch)гҖҒ TCOM (test-branch)гҖҒ YCOM (transliterate)гҖҒ ACOM (append) гҒӘгҒ©гҖӮ PTRSIZE=100 гҒ§жңҖеӨ§ 100 е‘Ҫд»ӨвҖ”вҖ”1975 е№ҙгҒ®зҸҫе®ҹзҡ„дёҠйҷҗгҖӮ ed (гӮӨгғігӮҝгғ©гӮҜгғҶгӮЈгғ–) гҒЁгҒ®ж №жң¬е·®гҒҜгҖҢгӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ«гҖҚ гҒҷгӮӢзӮ№гҖҒ еҫҢгҒ® awk/perl/Python pandas DSL гҒ®зҘ–гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Two-phase architecture: fcomp() in sed0.c reads sed script (from -f file, -e inline, or first arg) and compiles to ptrspace[]. execute() in sed1.c opens each input file and loops: read line via gline() into linebuf (pattern space), iterate all instructions checking addresses and running commands, auto-print pattern space unless -n. Memory stays constant: linebuf=4KB, holdsp=4KB, ptrspace=100Г—~50bytes=5KB, regex space=5KB. Total ~18KB regardless of input file size. The key insight: compile once, stream forever. Process 10GB file with fixed memory. This is the Unix pipeline DNA: each filter (grep/sed/awk/sort/uniq) does one job in constant memory, composed via |.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why can you `cat huge.log | sed 's/foo/bar/' | grep error | sort | uniq` and process terabytes of data on a laptop? Because each pipeline stage maintains constant memory. sed pioneered this design: compile the script once, then stream input lines through fixed-size buffers. 1975's Lee McMahon designed 'compile + stream' as the model for text filters. The whole Unix shell-fu culture вҖ” chained one-liners processing arbitrary data вҖ” depends on every filter behaving like sed: stateless across lines, memory-bounded, composable. Stream editing is fundamental to how Unix scales.

appraiser_note: **2 гғ•гӮ§гғјгӮәгӮўгғјгӮӯгғҶгӮҜгғҒгғЈ**: sed0.c гҒ® fcomp() гҒҢ sed гӮ№гӮҜгғӘгғ—гғҲ (-f гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«гҖҒ -e гӮӨгғігғ©гӮӨгғігҖҒ гҒҫгҒҹгҒҜжңҖеҲқгҒ®еј•ж•°) гӮ’иӘӯгӮ“гҒ§ ptrspace[] гҒ«гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ«гҖҒ sed1.c гҒ® execute() гҒҢеҗ„е…ҘеҠӣгғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«гӮ’й–ӢгҒ„гҒҰ: gline() гҒ§ 1 иЎҢгӮ’ linebuf (pattern space) гҒ«иӘӯгӮҖгҖҒ е…Ёе‘Ҫд»ӨгӮ’й Ҷж¬Ўи©•дҫЎ (гӮўгғүгғ¬гӮ№ вҶ’ гӮігғһгғігғүе®ҹиЎҢ)гҖҒ -n гғ•гғ©гӮ°з„ЎгҒ‘гӮҢгҒ° pattern space гӮ’иҮӘеӢ• printгҖӮ гғЎгғўгғӘдёҖе®ҡ: linebuf=4KBгҖҒ holdsp=4KBгҖҒ ptrspace=100Г—~50гғҗгӮӨгғҲ=5KBгҖҒ regex space=5KBгҖҒ еҗҲиЁҲ ~18KBвҖ”вҖ”е…ҘеҠӣгғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«гӮөгӮӨгӮәгҒ«з„Ўй–ўдҝӮгҖӮ **гҖҢгӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ«дёҖеәҰгҖҒ гӮ№гғҲгғӘгғјгғ ж°ёд№…гҖҚ** гҒ®зҷәжғівҖ”вҖ”10GB гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«гӮ’еӣәе®ҡгғЎгғўгғӘгҒ§еҮҰзҗҶгҖӮ Unix pipeline DNA: еҗ„гғ•гӮЈгғ«гӮҝ (grep/sed/awk/sort/uniq) гҒҢдёҖе®ҡгғЎгғўгғӘгҒ§дёҖд»•дәӢгҖҒ | гҒ§зөҗеҗҲгҖӮ `cat huge.log | sed | grep | sort | uniq` гҒҢгғҺгғјгғҲ PC гҒ§еӢ•гҒҸгҒ®гҒҜгҖҒ sed гҒҢ 1975 е№ҙгҒ«зўәз«ӢгҒ—гҒҹиЁӯиЁҲгҒ®гҒҠгҒӢгҒ’гҖӮ

text:
/* sed.h: */
#define CBRA   1   /* same as ed/grep */
#define CCHR   2   /* same */
#define CDOT   4   /* same */
#define CCL    6   /* same */
#define CNL    8   /* sed extension (ed has NCCL=8) */
#define CDOL  10   /* same */
#define CEOF  11   /* same */
#define CKET  12   /* same */
#define CNULL 13   /* sed extension */
#define CLNUM 14   /* sed: line number address */
#define CEND  16   /* sed: $ end-of-input */
#define CDONT 17   /* sed extension */
#define CBACK 18

#define STAR  01

long tlno[NLINES];     /* 256 line-number table */
int nlno;

ai_analysis.technical_context: sed shares regex opcodes with ed (#167) and grep (#166): CCHR=2, CDOT=4, CCL=6, CDOL=10, CEOF=11, CKET=12, CBRA=1, STAR=01. New additions: CLNUM (line-number address like `5d` for delete line 5), CEND ($ as end-of-input rather than end-of-pattern), CNL (newline anchor for multi-line patterns), CNULL/CDONT (internal optimization markers). tlno[256] stores actual line numbers referenced by CLNUM вҖ” execute() looks up `lnum != tlno[c]` to check if current line matches. Note: CNL=8 conflicts with ed's NCCL=8 (negated char class) вҖ” sed prefers stream-processing primitives over ed's edit-buffer ones. The shared base + sed-specific extensions show evolutionary regex design.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why do ed, grep, and sed all use regex constants CCHR=2, CDOT=4, CCL=6? Because they share lineage. ed (1971) wrote the regex engine; grep (1973) forked it; sed (1975) inherited the same numbers. But sed added stream-specific opcodes: CLNUM for 'line 5', CEND for 'last line of input'. The line-number table tlno[256] is sed-only вҖ” ed already knows line numbers via the editor buffer, grep doesn't care, but sed needs them because the user writes `1,$p` or `100d` in the script. The shared+extended design lets sed reuse 90% of Thompson's regex while adding the 10% that stream editing demands.

appraiser_note: sed гҒҜ ed (#167) гҒЁ grep (#166) гҒЁ regex opcode гӮ’е…ұжңү: CCHR=2, CDOT=4, CCL=6, CDOL=10, CEOF=11, CKET=12, CBRA=1, STAR=01гҖӮ sed зӢ¬иҮӘгҒ®жӢЎејө: **CLNUM** (иЎҢз•ӘеҸ·гӮўгғүгғ¬гӮ№гҖҒ `5d` гҒ§ 5 иЎҢзӣ®еүҠйҷӨ)гҖҒ **CEND** ($ гӮ’гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғіжң«е°ҫгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸ **е…ҘеҠӣжң«е°ҫ**гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰ)гҖҒ **CNL** (ж”№иЎҢгӮўгғігӮ«гғјгҖҒ иӨҮж•°иЎҢгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғіз”Ё)гҖҒ CNULL/CDONT (еҶ…йғЁжңҖйҒ©еҢ–)гҖӮ **tlno[256]** гҒҢ CLNUM еҸӮз…§гҒ®е®ҹйҡӣгҒ®иЎҢз•ӘеҸ·гӮ’дҝқеӯҳвҖ”вҖ”execute() гҒҢ `lnum != tlno[c]` гҒ§гӮ«гғ¬гғігғҲиЎҢгҒҢи©ІеҪ“гҒҷгӮӢгҒӢгғҒгӮ§гғғгӮҜгҖӮ жіЁж„Ҹ: CNL=8 гҒҜ ed гҒ® NCCL=8 (еҗҰе®ҡ char class) гҒЁиЎқзӘҒвҖ”вҖ”sed гҒҜгӮ№гғҲгғӘгғјгғ еҮҰзҗҶгғ—гғӘгғҹгғҶгӮЈгғ–гӮ’ ed гҒ®з·ЁйӣҶгғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎз”Ё opcode гӮҲгӮҠе„Әе…ҲгҖӮ е…ұжңүгғҷгғјгӮ№ + sed еӣәжңүжӢЎејөгҒ§гҖҒ Thompson regex гҒ® 90% гӮ’еҶҚеҲ©з”ЁгҒ—гҒӘгҒҢгӮү stream editing еӣәжңүгҒ® 10% гӮ’иҝҪеҠ гҖӮ
