ai_analysis.technical_context: sed has TWO buffers, not one: pattern space (linebuf, holds current line being edited) and hold space (holdsp, scratch buffer). Commands move data between them: h (hold=pattern), H (hold+=pattern), g (pattern=hold), G (pattern+=hold), x (swap). This enables multi-line processing despite sed's per-line streaming model. Hold space lets sed remember context: previous line, accumulated data, intermediate results. Example: `1!G;h;$!d` reverses a file by accumulating reversed-order lines in hold space. Example: collapsing blank lines uses hold space to track 'was previous line blank?'. Both buffers are 4000 bytes вҖ” sufficient for any line up to 4KB, accumulating maybe dozens of lines. GNU sed extends to dynamic sizing; classic sed's 4000-byte cap is fixed.
ai_analysis.human_story: sed processes one line at a time вҖ” how does it handle multi-line operations? Answer: hold space. A second 4KB buffer alongside the pattern space. 5 commands move data between them (h H g G x). Want to remember the previous line? `h` saves it. Want to swap current and saved? `x`. Want to print every other line? Use hold space to track parity. Want to reverse the file? Accumulate lines in hold space with `H`, then print at the end. Hold space turns stateless line-processing into stateful sequential-processing, in 5 commands. The 1975 design is still teaching us 'state in a fixed-size scratch buffer' вҖ” the same pattern as register-based VMs, hardware accumulators, calculator displays.

appraiser_note: sed гҒҜ **2 гҒӨгҒ®гғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎ**гӮ’жҢҒгҒӨ: pattern space (linebufгҖҒ зҸҫеңЁз·ЁйӣҶдёӯгҒ®иЎҢ) + hold space (holdspгҖҒ гӮ№гӮҜгғ©гғғгғҒгғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎ)гҖӮ 5 гҒӨгҒ®гӮігғһгғігғүгҒ§дёЎиҖ…й–“гҒ®гғҮгғјгӮҝз§»еӢ•: **h** (hold=pattern)гҖҒ **H** (hold+=pattern)гҖҒ **g** (pattern=hold)гҖҒ **G** (pattern+=hold)гҖҒ **x** (swap)гҖӮ sed гҒ® per-line streaming гғўгғҮгғ«гҒ«гӮӮгҒӢгҒӢгӮҸгӮүгҒҡ **иӨҮж•°иЎҢеҮҰзҗҶ**гҒҢеҸҜиғҪгҖӮ hold space гҒ§ж–Үи„ҲгӮ’иЁҳжҶ¶: еүҚгҒ®иЎҢгҖҒ зҙҜз©ҚгғҮгғјгӮҝгҖҒ дёӯй–“зөҗжһңгҖӮ дҫӢ: `1!G;h;$!d` гҒ§йҖҶй ҶиЎЁзӨә (hold space гҒ«йҖҶй Ҷи“„з©Қ)гҖҒ `/./,/^$/{/^$/h;/./{x;/^$/d}}` гҒ§йҖЈз¶ҡз©әиЎҢгӮ’ 1 иЎҢгҒ«гҖӮ дёЎгғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎгҒЁгӮӮ 4000 гғҗгӮӨгғҲвҖ”вҖ”4KB д»ҘеҶ…гҒ®д»»ж„ҸгҒ®иЎҢгҖҒ ж•°еҚҒиЎҢеҲҶгҒ®зҙҜз©ҚгҒ«еҚҒеҲҶгҖӮ GNU sed гҒҜеӢ•зҡ„гӮөгӮӨгӮәгҒ гҒҢеҸӨе…ё sed гҒҜ 4000 гғҗгӮӨгғҲеӣәе®ҡгҖӮ 1975 е№ҙгҒ®иЁӯиЁҲгҒҢгҖҢ**еӣәе®ҡгӮөгӮӨгӮәгӮ№гӮҜгғ©гғғгғҒгғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎгҒ«зҠ¶ж…Ӣ**гҖҚ гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶгҖҒ гғ¬гӮёгӮ№гӮҝ VM/гғҸгғјгғүгӮҰгӮ§гӮўгӮўгӮӯгғҘгғ гғ¬гғјгӮҝ/йӣ»еҚ“иЎЁзӨәгҒЁеҗҢгҒҳгғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігӮ’ж•ҷгҒҲгӮӢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: sed has goto: `b label` (unconditional branch) and `t label` (test branch вҖ” jumps only after successful substitution). `:label` defines a target. ltab[50] is the label table (8-char names). reptr2's lb1 field holds the resolved jump target (filled by dechain() at end of fcomp). At execute time, command() sets jflag=1 on branch, and execute() checks jflag to set `ipc = ipc->lb1`. The b without label jumps to script end (skip remaining commands for this line). With labels + branches + regex matching + 5 buffer commands (h/H/g/G/x), sed is Turing-complete: it can implement addition (in unary), prime checking, even Conway's Life. Most users only ever write 1-line sed scripts, but the language capability is full.
ai_analysis.human_story: Did you know sed is Turing-complete? With `b label` (jump), `t label` (jump-if-substitution-succeeded), labels (`:foo`), regex matching, and the 5 buffer commands, you can compute anything. Someone implemented Conway's Life in sed. Someone wrote a sed prime sieve. Lee McMahon in 1975 didn't intend sed to be a general programming language вҖ” but adding labels and branches made it one. Most people use sed for `sed 's/old/new/'` one-liners, never touching `b`/`t`. But the capability is there. The lesson: small composable primitives (matching + state buffers + jumps) can produce universal computation. sed is the smallest accidentally-Turing-complete Unix tool.

appraiser_note: sed гҒ«гҒҜ **goto** гҒҢгҒӮгӮӢ: `b label` (з„ЎжқЎд»¶еҲҶеІҗ) гҒЁ `t label` (гғҶгӮ№гғҲеҲҶеІҗвҖ”вҖ”`s///` жҲҗеҠҹжҷӮгҒ®гҒҝгӮёгғЈгғігғ—)гҖӮ `:label` гҒ§гӮҝгғјгӮІгғғгғҲе®ҡзҫ©гҖӮ ltab[50] гҒҢгғ©гғҷгғ«гғҶгғјгғ–гғ« (8 ж–Үеӯ—еҗҚ)гҖӮ reptr2 гҒ® lb1 гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғүгҒҢи§ЈжұәжёҲгҒҝгӮёгғЈгғігғ—гӮҝгғјгӮІгғғгғҲгӮ’дҝқжҢҒ (fcomp жң«е°ҫгҒ® dechain() гҒ§и§Јжұә)гҖӮ е®ҹиЎҢжҷӮ command() гҒҢеҲҶеІҗгҒ§ jflag=1 гӮ’гӮ»гғғгғҲгҖҒ execute() гҒҢ jflag гӮ’иҰӢгҒҰ `ipc = ipc->lb1`гҖӮ гғ©гғҷгғ«з„ЎгҒ—гҒ® b гҒҜгӮ№гӮҜгғӘгғ—гғҲжң«е°ҫгҒёгӮёгғЈгғігғ— (гҒ“гҒ®иЎҢгҒ®ж®ӢгӮҠгӮігғһгғігғүгӮ’гӮ№гӮӯгғғгғ—)гҖӮ гғ©гғҷгғ« + еҲҶеІҗ + regex гғһгғғгғҒ + 5 гҒӨгҒ®гғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎгӮігғһгғігғү (h/H/g/G/x) гҒ§ sed гҒҜ **Turing е®ҢеӮҷ**вҖ”вҖ”еҠ з®— (1 йҖІжі•)гҖҒ зҙ ж•°гғҒгӮ§гғғгӮҜгҖҒ гӮігғігӮҰгӮ§гӮӨгҒ®гғ©гӮӨгғ•гӮІгғјгғ гҒҫгҒ§е®ҹиЈ…еҸҜиғҪгҖӮ еӨҡгҒҸгҒ®гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјгҒҜ 1 иЎҢ sed гҒ—гҒӢжӣёгҒӢгҒӘгҒ„гҒҢгҖҒ иЁҖиӘһгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰгҒҜе®Ңе…ЁгҖӮ Lee McMahon гҒҜ 1975 е№ҙгҒ« sed гӮ’жұҺз”ЁиЁҖиӘһгҒ«гҒҷгӮӢж°—гҒҜз„ЎгҒӢгҒЈгҒҹгҒҢгҖҒ гғ©гғҷгғ« + еҲҶеІҗгҒ®иҝҪеҠ гҒ§ж„ҸеӣігҒӣгҒҡ Turing е®ҢеӮҷгҒ«гҒӘгҒЈгҒҹгҖӮ гҖҢ**е°ҸгҒ•гҒӘеҗҲжҲҗеҸҜиғҪгғ—гғӘгғҹгғҶгӮЈгғ– (гғһгғғгғҒ + зҠ¶ж…Ӣгғҗгғғгғ•гӮЎ + гӮёгғЈгғігғ—)** гҒ§жҷ®йҒҚиЁҲз®—гҒҢеҸҜиғҪгҖҚ гҒЁгҒ„гҒҶж•ҷиЁ“гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: awk.g.y defines a complete programming language in 269 lines of yacc grammar. Tokens cover comparison operators (LT/LE/GT/GE/EQ/NE), match operators (MATCH/NOTMATCH = ~ !~), arithmetic (ADD/MINUS/MULT/DIVIDE/MOD), assignment (ASSIGN/ADDEQ/SUBEQ/MULTEQ/DIVEQ/MODEQ), control flow (IF/ELSE/WHILE/FOR/IN/NEXT/EXIT/BREAK/CONTINUE), I/O (PRINT/PRINTF/SPRINTF), built-in functions (SPLIT/SUBSTR/INDEX/MATCH), pre/post inc-dec (POSTINCR/PREINCR/POSTDECR/PREDECR). Operator precedence cascade from low (ASGNOP) to high (FIELD INDIRECT). Uses lex tokenizer (awk.lx.l) вҖ” yacc + lex applied seriously, unlike ed/grep/sed's hand-written parsers. Aho was co-author of the Dragon Book (Compilers: Principles, Techniques, Tools) вҖ” awk is his reference implementation of compiler theory.
ai_analysis.human_story: ed, grep, sed all hand-rolled their parsers. awk did it with yacc + lex вҖ” using compiler theory seriously. Why? Because awk needed to be a real programming language with proper operator precedence, not a one-letter command interpreter. Alfred Aho, one of awk's three authors, co-wrote the Dragon Book вҖ” the standard compiler textbook. awk is, in a sense, Aho's compiler-theory in action. 269 lines of yacc express what would take 1000+ lines of recursive-descent parser. Lex tokenizes, yacc parses, run.c interprets вҖ” a clean compiler pipeline that became the model for Perl, PHP, Python, Ruby all building proper grammar.

appraiser_note: awk.g.y гҒҜ **269 иЎҢгҒ® yacc ж–Үжі•**гҒ§е®Ңе…Ёгғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғҹгғігӮ°иЁҖиӘһгӮ’е®ҡзҫ©гҖӮ гғҲгғјгӮҜгғі: жҜ”ијғжј”з®—еӯҗ (LT/LE/GT/GE/EQ/NE)гҖҒ гғһгғғгғҒжј”з®—еӯҗ (MATCH/NOTMATCH = ~ !~)гҖҒ з®—иЎ“ (ADD/MINUS/MULT/DIVIDE/MOD)гҖҒ д»Је…Ҙ (ASSIGN/ADDEQ/SUBEQ/MULTEQ/DIVEQ/MODEQ)гҖҒ еҲ¶еҫЎгғ•гғӯгғј (IF/ELSE/WHILE/FOR/IN/NEXT/EXIT/BREAK/CONTINUE)гҖҒ I/O (PRINT/PRINTF/SPRINTF)гҖҒ зө„гҒҝиҫјгҒҝй–ўж•° (SPLIT/SUBSTR/INDEX/MATCH)гҖҒ еүҚ/еҫҢзҪ® inc-decгҖӮ жј”з®—еӯҗе„Әе…Ҳй ҶдҪҚгӮ«гӮ№гӮұгғјгғү: жңҖдҪҺ ASGNOP вҶ’ жңҖй«ҳ FIELD INDIRECTгҖӮ lex гғҲгғјгӮҜгғҠгӮӨгӮ¶ (awk.lx.l) гҒЁзө„гҒҝеҗҲгӮҸгҒӣвҖ”вҖ”ed/grep/sed гҒ®жүӢжӣёгҒҚгғ‘гғјгӮөгҒЁйҒ•гҒ„гҖҒ **yacc + lex гӮ’жң¬ж°—гҒ§йҒ©з”Ё**гҖӮ Aho гҒҜ Dragon Book (Compilers: Principles, Techniques, Tools) гҒ®е…ұи‘—иҖ…вҖ”вҖ”awk гҒҜ **еҪјгҒ®гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ©зҗҶи«–гҒ®еҸӮиҖғе®ҹиЈ…**гҖӮ 269 иЎҢгҒ® yacc гҒҢеҶҚеё°дёӢйҷҚгғ‘гғјгӮө 1000+ иЎҢгҒ«зӣёеҪ“гҖӮ еҫҢгҒ® Perl/PHP/Python/Ruby гҒҢ proper grammar гҒ§жӣёгҒҸжөҒжҙҫгҒ®зҘ–гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: awk's pattern-action paradigm: program = BEGIN block + pattern-action statements + END block. Each pa_stat = pattern + action block. Runtime: BEGIN runs once before input, then for each input line iterate all pa_stats (if pattern matches, run action), END runs once after input. Patterns can be /regex/, expressions ($3 > 100), ranges (/start/,/end/), or compound (pat1 && pat2). Actions are statement blocks with full language features (if/while/for/print/arrays). PASTAT AST node holds (pattern, action) pair. This extends sed's /regex/ command model into structured-language scope. Data-driven programming: declare what to do per line type, not how to iterate. Modern descendants: SQL SELECT WHERE, pandas .apply, MapReduce mappers, Spark DataFrame operations.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why does `awk '{print $1}'` feel different from `sed s/foo/bar/`? Because awk is declarative: 'for each line, do this'. sed is closer to a command stream. awk's pattern-action model says 'declare what to do when X matches'. BEGIN initializes, patterns trigger actions, END summarizes. This is the data-driven programming pattern: structure code around 'what input shapes deserve what response'. SQL's SELECT...WHERE..., pandas's df.apply(), MapReduce's mappers, Spark's filter+map вҖ” all express the same idea: declarative per-record processing. awk pioneered it in 1977 for text streams.

appraiser_note: awk гҒ® **pattern-action гғ‘гғ©гғҖгӮӨгғ **: гғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғ  = BEGIN гғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜ + pattern-action ж–ҮгҒ®еҲ— + END гғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜгҖӮ еҗ„ pa_stat = pattern + action гғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜгҖӮ гғ©гғігӮҝгӮӨгғ : BEGIN гӮ’ 1 еӣһе®ҹиЎҢ вҶ’ еҗ„е…ҘеҠӣиЎҢгҒ§е…Ё pa_stats гӮ’й Ҷж¬Ўи©•дҫЎ (pattern дёҖиҮҙгҒӘгӮү action) вҶ’ END гӮ’ 1 еӣһе®ҹиЎҢгҖӮ гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒҜ /regex/гҖҒ ејҸ ($3 > 100)гҖҒ зҜ„еӣІ (/start/,/end/)гҖҒ иӨҮеҗҲ (pat1 && pat2)гҖӮ гӮўгӮҜгӮ·гғ§гғігҒҜиӨҮж–Ү (if/while/for/print/array)гҖӮ PASTAT AST гғҺгғјгғүгҒҢ (pattern, action) гғҡгӮўгӮ’дҝқжҢҒгҖӮ sed гҒ® /regex/ command гӮ’ **ж§ӢйҖ еҢ–иЁҖиӘһгҒ®гӮ№гӮігғјгғ—**гҒ«жӢЎејөгҖӮ **гғҮгғјгӮҝй§ҶеӢ•еһӢгғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғҹгғігӮ°**: гҖҢгҒ©гҒҶз№°гӮҠиҝ”гҒҷгҒӢгҖҚ гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸ гҖҢгҒ©гҒ®е…ҘеҠӣгҒ«дҪ•гӮ’гҒҷгӮӢгҒӢгҖҚ гӮ’е®ЈиЁҖгҖӮ еҫҢгҒ® SQL SELECT WHEREгҖҒ pandas .applyгҖҒ MapReduce mapperгҖҒ Spark DataFrame operations гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰеҗҢгҒҳжҖқжғівҖ”вҖ”awk гҒҢ 1977 е№ҙгҒ«гғҶгӮӯгӮ№гғҲгӮ№гғҲгғӘгғјгғ гҒ§е…Ҳй§ҶгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: awk's Cell struct holds both string (sval) and numeric (fval) values with a type flag (tval) indicating which is current. getfval auto-converts string to double via atof; getsval auto-converts double to string via sprintf %g. `"42" + 1` works because + forces numeric context, triggering getfval. `42 " lines"` (juxtaposition = concat in awk) forces string context. Field splitting: readrec reads a line, sets $0, then walks FS-separated fields setting $1, $2, ..., updating NF and NR. Field separator FS defaults to whitespace, customizable (e.g. FS=":"). $NF, $(NF-1) work because field references are evaluated. This dynamic typing + automatic field splitting is awk's killer ergonomics: type as little as possible to process structured text.
ai_analysis.human_story: Type `awk '{print $1+$2}'` on a file of two numbers per line вҖ” it works. The fields $1 and $2 are strings (from text input) but + makes awk convert them to numbers. Type `awk '{print $1".txt"}'` вҖ” concatenation, $1 stays a string. The 1977 awk made dynamic typing transparent: declare nothing, awk figures out from context. Combined with automatic field splitting (`$0`, `$1`, `$NF`), awk reads /etc/passwd more naturally than C: `awk -F: '{print $1, $5}'`. Perl, PHP, Python, JavaScript all inherit awk's principle: 'string vs number, just do the right thing'. The Cell struct holding both representations is 1977's gift to modern scripting.

appraiser_note: awk гҒ® Cell ж§ӢйҖ дҪ“гҒҜ **ж–Үеӯ—еҲ— (sval) гҒЁж•°еҖӨ (fval) гҒ®дёЎж–№**гӮ’дҝқжҢҒгҒ—гҖҒ еһӢгғ•гғ©гӮ° (tval) гҒ§зҸҫеңЁгҒ®еҪўејҸгӮ’зӨәгҒҷгҖӮ `getfval()` гҒҜ atof гҒ§ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—вҶ’double иҮӘеӢ•еӨүжҸӣгҖҒ `getsval()` гҒҜ sprintf %g гҒ§ doubleвҶ’ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гҖӮ `"42" + 1` гҒҢеӢ•гҒҸгҒ®гҒҜ + гҒҢж•°еҖӨгӮігғігғҶгӮӯгӮ№гғҲгӮ’еј·еҲ¶гҒ— getfval гӮ’гғҲгғӘгӮ¬гғјгҒҷгӮӢгҒӢгӮүгҖҒ `42 " lines"` (awk гҒ® juxtaposition = йҖЈзөҗ) гҒҜж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮігғігғҶгӮӯгӮ№гғҲгӮ’еј·еҲ¶гҖӮ **гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғүеҲҶеүІ**: readrec гҒҢ 1 иЎҢиӘӯгӮ“гҒ§ $0 иЁӯе®ҡгҖҒ FS еҢәеҲҮгӮҠгҒ§ $1, $2, ... гӮ’иЁӯе®ҡгҖҒ NF гҒЁ NR жӣҙж–°гҖӮ FS гғҮгғ•гӮ©гғ«гғҲгҒҜз©әзҷҪгҖҒ гӮ«гӮ№гӮҝгғһгӮӨгӮәеҸҜ (`FS=":"`)гҖӮ $NF, $(NF-1) гӮӮи©•дҫЎеҸҜиғҪгҖӮ еӢ•зҡ„еһӢ + иҮӘеӢ•гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғүеҲҶеүІ = awk гҒ®гӮӯгғ©гғјгӮЁгғ«гӮҙгғҺгғҹгӮҜгӮ№: **гҖҢж§ӢйҖ еҢ–гғҶгӮӯгӮ№гғҲеҮҰзҗҶгҒ«жңҖе°ҸйҷҗгҒ®гӮҝгӮӨгғ”гғігӮ°**гҖҚгҖӮ Perl/PHP/Python/JavaScript гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰ awk гҒ®гҖҢж–Үеӯ—еҲ— vs ж•°еҖӨгҖҒ ж–Үи„ҲгҒ§еҲӨж–ӯгҖҚ еҺҹеүҮгӮ’з¶ҷжүҝгҖӮ Cell ж§ӢйҖ дҪ“гҒҜ 1977 е№ҙгҒ®зҸҫд»ЈгӮ№гӮҜгғӘгғ—гғҲгҒёгҒ®иҙҲгӮҠзү©гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: awk associative arrays: a["key"] = value where key is any string. Internally implemented as hash table (symtab[]) with dynamic sizing and chaining for collisions (tran.c). Index expression is evaluated, stringified, looked up or created in the array's symtab. `for (key in array)` iterates all keys. Multi-dimensional via SUBSEP separator: `a[i, j]` becomes `a[i SUBSEP j]` (default SUBSEP = \034 = ASCII FS). APL/SNOBOL/Lisp had associative data structures, but they were for 'programmer' languages. awk brought hash tables to 'Unix user' tool вҖ” natural syntax, no setup, just `a[key] = value`. Influence: Perl %hash, PHP $array, Python dict, JavaScript object, Ruby Hash вҖ” all descend from awk's syntax and semantics.
ai_analysis.human_story: Before awk, hash tables were a 'programmer' data structure вҖ” you used them in Lisp or wrote your own. After awk (1977), `a["key"] = value` was a one-liner for shell users. Want to count word occurrences? `awk '{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++)c[$i]++} END{for(w in c)print w,c[w]}'`. Want to group log lines by date? Same pattern. The hash table вҖ” that staple of every modern language вҖ” was popularized for everyday text processing by awk. Multi-dim `a[i,j]` via SUBSEP separator anticipated Python tuple keys `d[(i,j)]`. The 1977 design pattern of 'arrays-with-string-keys' is the foundation of dict in every scripting language since.

appraiser_note: awk гҒ®йҖЈжғій…ҚеҲ—: `a["key"] = value`гҖҒ гӮӯгғјгҒҜд»»ж„ҸгҒ®ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гҖӮ еҶ…йғЁе®ҹиЈ…гҒҜ **hash table (symtab[])**гҖҒ еӢ•зҡ„гӮөгӮӨгӮә + chaining (tran.c)гҖӮ гӮӨгғігғҮгғғгӮҜгӮ№ејҸгӮ’и©•дҫЎ вҶ’ ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—еҢ– вҶ’ й…ҚеҲ—гҒ® symtab гҒ§ lookup or createгҖӮ `for (key in array)` гҒ§е…ЁгӮӯгғјиө°жҹ»гҖӮ еӨҡж¬Ўе…ғгҒҜ **SUBSEP** гҒ§йҖЈзөҗ: `a[i, j]` гҒҜ `a[i SUBSEP j]` (гғҮгғ•гӮ©гғ«гғҲ SUBSEP = \034 = ASCII FS)гҖӮ APL/SNOBOL/Lisp гӮӮйҖЈжғіж§ӢйҖ гӮ’жҢҒгҒӨгҒҢгҖҢгғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғһиЁҖиӘһгҖҚ гҒ гҒЈгҒҹгҖӮ awk гҒҢ **Unix гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјгғ„гғјгғ«**гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰ hash table гӮ’жҢҒгҒЎиҫјгҒҝвҖ”вҖ”иҮӘз„¶гҒӘж§Ӣж–ҮгҖҒ гӮ»гғғгғҲгӮўгғғгғ—дёҚиҰҒгҖҒ `a[key] = value` гҒ®гҒҝгҖӮ еҪұйҹҝ: Perl %hashгҖҒ PHP $arrayгҖҒ Python dictгҖҒ JavaScript objectгҖҒ Ruby HashвҖ”вҖ”гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰ awk гҒ®ж§Ӣж–ҮгҒЁж„Ҹе‘іи«–гӮ’з¶ҷжүҝгҖӮ 1977 е№ҙгҒ®гҖҢж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮӯгғјй…ҚеҲ—гҖҚ гғҮгӮ¶гӮӨгғігғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒҢгҖҒ е…ЁгӮ№гӮҜгғӘгғ—гғҲиЁҖиӘһгҒ® dict гҒ®еҹәзӣӨгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: MATCHOP (~ !~) is a binary operator: lhs is string expression, rhs is regex. `$1 ~ /^abc/` evaluates $1 then matches against /^abc/. Crucially, the regex side can be a variable: `$0 ~ pattern` where pattern is a string. This makes regex first-class values, not just literal patterns. makedfa() compiles regex to DFA (decision-free automaton) вҖ” implemented in b.c, separate from ed/grep/sed's NFA backtracking engine. DFA gives linear-time O(n) matching with no backtracking, no exponential blow-up вҖ” at the cost of not supporting backreferences. awk chose DFA because data processing requires worst-case guarantees. Later GNU grep, RE2 (Google), Rust regex all chose DFA for the same reason. Programmable regex via variables enables data-driven matching: pattern tables, runtime-constructed patterns, regex-as-config.
ai_analysis.human_story: awk made regex a first-class value. `$1 ~ /pattern/` is a boolean expression you can use anywhere вҖ” in if conditions, in compound patterns, in variable assignments. The regex can come from a variable: `pattern = read_config(); if ($0 ~ pattern) ...`. ed/grep/sed treat regex as static syntax; awk treats it as data. The b.c regex engine uses DFA (not NFA backtracking) for linear-time guaranteed performance вҖ” essential when processing GBs of log data. Modern DFA engines (RE2, Rust regex, GNU grep -F) descend from this 1977 choice. The MATCHOP operator's syntax (`~` and `!~`) survives in Perl, Ruby, and many other languages.

appraiser_note: **MATCHOP (~ !~)** гҒҜдәҢй …жј”з®—еӯҗ: lhs гҒҜж–Үеӯ—еҲ—ејҸгҖҒ rhs гҒҜ regexгҖӮ `$1 ~ /^abc/` гҒҜ $1 гӮ’и©•дҫЎгҒ—гҒҰ /^abc/ гҒЁгғһгғғгғҒгҖӮ **regex еҒҙгӮ’еӨүж•°гҒ«гҒ§гҒҚгӮӢ**: `$0 ~ pattern` (pattern гҒҜж–Үеӯ—еҲ—)гҖӮ гҒ“гӮҢгҒ§ **regex гҒҢз¬¬дёҖзҙҡгҒ®еҖӨ**гҒ«гҒӘгӮӢгҖҒ еҚҳгҒӘгӮӢгғӘгғҶгғ©гғ«гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ makedfa() гҒҢ regex гӮ’ **DFA (жұәе®ҡжҖ§жңүйҷҗгӮӘгғјгғҲгғһгғҲгғі)** гҒ«гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ«вҖ”вҖ”b.c гҒ§е®ҹиЈ…гҖҒ ed/grep/sed гҒ® NFA backtracking гҒЁгҒҜеҲҘгҖӮ DFA гҒҜ **з·ҡеҪўжҷӮй–“ O(n) дҝқиЁј**гҖҒ backtrack/exponential blow-up гҒӘгҒ—вҖ”вҖ”гғҗгғғгӮҜгғӘгғ•гӮЎгғ¬гғігӮ№дёҚеҸҜгҒ®д»ЈгӮҸгӮҠгҒ«гҖӮ awk гҒҢ DFA гӮ’йҒёгӮ“гҒ гҒ®гҒҜ **гғҮгғјгӮҝеҮҰзҗҶгҒ« worst-case дҝқиЁјгҒҢеҝ…иҰҒ**гҒ гҒӢгӮүгҖӮ еҫҢгҒ® GNU grepгҖҒ RE2 (Google)гҖҒ Rust regex гӮӮеҗҢгҒҳзҗҶз”ұгҒ§ DFA жҺЎз”ЁгҖӮ еӨүж•°зөҢз”ұгҒ®гғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғһгғ–гғ« regex гҒ§ **гғҮгғјгӮҝй§ҶеӢ•гғһгғғгғҒгғігӮ°**гҒҢеҸҜиғҪ: гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғіиЎЁгҖҒ е®ҹиЎҢжҷӮж§ӢзҜүгҖҒ regex-as-configгҖӮ MATCHOP ж§Ӣж–Ү (`~`/`!~`) гҒҜ Perl/Ruby/д»–иЁҖиӘһгҒ«гӮӮз¶ҷжүҝгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: echo.c is 23 lines total including #include. K&R-style C: argc/argv K&R declaration form, register hint for tight loop, nflg int as boolean counter, fputs+putchar rather than printf (avoids format string parsing overhead and vulnerability), exit(0) explicit even though main returning 0 would be equivalent (some pre-C89 compilers didn't honor main's return value). Function does: skip -n if present, fputs each remaining arg with space separator (no trailing space), final newline unless -n. Compiled binary ~1KB. Uses only fputs/putchar/exit from libc вҖ” minimal runtime dependency. The 23 lines exemplify Unix's 'do one thing well': no malloc, no error handling beyond exit(0), no format-string interpretation. The simplest useful command.
ai_analysis.human_story: 23 lines for the most-used Unix command after ls. echo is the 'hello world' of Unix tools: it must exist, it must be tiny, it must just work. K&R-era programmers showed off by writing the minimum вҖ” register hints, int-as-bool, fputs instead of printf (faster, smaller, safer). The exit(0) is intentionally explicit because pre-C89 compilers couldn't reliably handle main's return value. Every byte of the 23 lines is deliberate. It compiles to ~1KB. Modern shells like bash provide echo as a builtin to avoid the fork/exec cost вҖ” but the spec they implement traces to these 23 lines.

appraiser_note: echo.c гҒҜ `#include` еҗ«гӮҒ **23 иЎҢ**гҖӮ K&R style C: argc/argv K&R е®ЈиЁҖгҖҒ register гғ’гғігғҲгҖҒ nflg int гӮ’ bool counter гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰгҖҒ fputs+putchar (printf и§ЈжһҗгӮӘгғјгғҗгғјгғҳгғғгғүгҒЁ format string и„ҶејұжҖ§еӣһйҒҝ)гҖҒ exit(0) жҳҺзӨә (C89 еүҚгҒ®гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ©гҒҜ main гҒ® return гӮ’ exit code гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰжүұгӮҸгҒӘгҒӢгҒЈгҒҹ)гҖӮ ж©ҹиғҪ: -n гҒӮгӮҢгҒ° skipгҖҒ еҗ„еј•ж•°гӮ’ fputs + з©әзҷҪеҢәеҲҮгӮҠ (жң«е°ҫгҒӘгҒ—)гҖҒ -n гҒӘгҒ—гҒӘгӮүжңҖеҫҢгҒ«ж”№иЎҢгҖӮ гғҗгӮӨгғҠгғӘ ~1KBгҖӮ libc дҫқеӯҳгҒҜ fputs/putchar/exit гҒ®гҒҝвҖ”вҖ”жңҖе°Ҹгғ©гғігӮҝгӮӨгғ дҫқеӯҳгҖӮ 23 иЎҢгҒҢ Unix гҒ® **гҖҢDo one thing wellгҖҚ** гӮ’дҪ“зҸҫ: malloc гҒӘгҒ—гҖҒ гӮЁгғ©гғјеҮҰзҗҶгҒҜ exit(0) гҒ®гҒҝгҖҒ format string и§ЈйҮҲгҒӘгҒ—гҖӮ **жңҖгӮӮеҚҳзҙ”гҒӘе®ҹз”ЁгӮігғһгғігғү**вҖ”вҖ”ls гҒ®ж¬ЎгҒ«гӮҲгҒҸдҪҝгӮҸгӮҢгӮӢ Unix йҒ“е…·гҒ§гҖҒ K&R жҷӮд»ЈгҒ®гғ—гғӯгӮ°гғ©гғһгҒҢгҖҢжңҖе°ҸгҒ§жӣёгҒҸгҖҚ гҒ“гҒЁгӮ’иӘҮзӨәгҒ—гҒҹе§ҝгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: Bell-32V echo (BSD lineage) interprets -n flag, doesn't process backslash escapes. System V echo does opposite: ignores -n (treats as literal arg), processes \n \t \b \c etc. POSIX 1003.1 couldn't unify and lets both behaviors stand. Result: portable shell scripts must avoid echo or test which version is present. GNU echo defaults BSD-style but adds -e to enable System V-style escapes, -E to force literal. bash builtin echo follows configurable xpg_echo shell option. The fork happened because System V wanted to add features (escape sequences), BSD wanted to add features (-n flag), and neither side compromised. The 1979 Bell-32V design is the BSD origin: 23 lines, -n yes, escapes no.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why is `echo -n hello` portable on macOS but prints "-n hello" on Solaris? Because echo split in two during the 1980s. Bell-32V (1979) had -n, no backslash escapes вҖ” BSD inherited this. System V (1983) added backslash escapes \n \t \c, dropped -n. POSIX gave up on standardizing. Scripts dependent on either behavior aren't portable. The lesson: when two designs disagree about a tiny command, the right answer might be 'use printf' вҖ” which is what POSIX recommends. echo's 23 lines spawned 40 years of shell-script portability headaches.

appraiser_note: Bell-32V echo (BSD зі») гҒҜ -n гғ•гғ©гӮ°гӮ’и§ЈйҮҲгҒ—гҖҒ гғҗгғғгӮҜгӮ№гғ©гғғгӮ·гғҘгӮЁгӮ№гӮұгғјгғ—гӮ’еҮҰзҗҶгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ System V echo гҒҜйҖҶ: -n гӮ’з„ЎиҰ– (гғӘгғҶгғ©гғ«еј•ж•°гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰжүұгҒҶ)гҖҒ \n \t \b \c гҒӘгҒ©гӮ’и§ЈйҮҲгҖӮ POSIX 1003.1 гҒҜзөұдёҖгҒ§гҒҚгҒҡ**дёЎж–№гӮ’иЁұе®№**гҖӮ зөҗжһң: еҸҜжҗ¬гӮ·гӮ§гғ«гӮ№гӮҜгғӘгғ—гғҲгҒҜ echo гӮ’йҒҝгҒ‘гӮӢгҒӢгҖҒ гҒ©гҒЎгӮүгҒ®зүҲгҒӢеҲӨе®ҡгҒҷгӮӢеҝ…иҰҒгҒҢгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ GNU echo гҒҜгғҮгғ•гӮ©гғ«гғҲ BSD зі»гҖҒ -e гҒ§ System V escape жңүеҠ№еҢ–гҖҒ -E гҒ§гғӘгғҶгғ©гғ«еј·еҲ¶гҖӮ bash builtin echo гҒҜ xpg_echo shell гӮӘгғ—гӮ·гғ§гғігҒ§жҢҷеӢ•еҲҮжӣҝгҖӮ fork гҒ®еҺҹеӣ : System V гҒҜ escape ж©ҹиғҪиҝҪеҠ гҖҒ BSD гҒҜ -n гғ•гғ©гӮ°иҝҪеҠ гӮ’йҒёгҒігҖҒ дә’гҒ„гҒ«еҰҘеҚ”гҒӣгҒҡгҖӮ 1979 е№ҙ Bell-32V гҒ®иЁӯиЁҲгҒҢ BSD иө·жәҗвҖ”вҖ”23 иЎҢгҖҒ -n гҒӮгӮҠгҖҒ escape гҒӘгҒ—гҖӮ `echo -n hello` гҒҢ macOS гҒ§еӢ•гҒҸгҒҢ Solaris гҒ§гҖҢ-n helloгҖҚ гҒЁеҮәгӮӢгҒ®гҒҜгҒ“гҒ®еҲҶеІҗгҒ®зөҗжһңгҖӮ 40 е№ҙгҒ® shell-script з§»жӨҚжҖ§е•ҸйЎҢгӮ’з”ҹгӮ“гҒ  23 иЎҢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: echo deliberately uses fputs/putchar instead of printf. Reasons: (1) Performance: printf scans format string at runtime; fputs/putchar skip this. (2) Code size: avoids linking the format-parsing portion of libc, keeping binary tiny (~1KB). (3) Safety: printf with user-controlled format string is the format-string-vulnerability classic (CVE staple); fputs has no format concept, so user input can't escape its data role. (4) Clarity: 'output this string' as `fputs(s, fd)` is more direct than `printf("%s", s)`. Modern GNU echo shares code with printf in coreutils as a side benefit, but the 1979 design separated them. The same fputs-over-printf principle applies in modern critical paths: logging fast paths, embedded systems, kernel printk variants.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why doesn't echo use printf? Three reasons: speed (no format parsing), size (no format module in libc), safety (no format-string injection). In 1979, the third reason wasn't even articulated as a security concept вҖ” format-string vulnerabilities became famous in the late 1990s. But Bell-32V's echo accidentally got it right by using fputs, which has no concept of format directives. Modern critical-path logging often follows the same pattern: log handlers prefer raw writes over printf for performance and safety. The 23-line echo applied 'use the right tool' wisdom decades before security researchers formalized it.

appraiser_note: echo гҒҜж„Ҹеӣізҡ„гҒ« printf гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸ **fputs/putchar** гӮ’дҪҝгҒҶгҖӮ зҗҶз”ұ: (1) **жҖ§иғҪ**: printf гҒҜгғ©гғігӮҝгӮӨгғ гҒ§ format ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮ’гӮ№гӮӯгғЈгғігҖҒ fputs/putchar гҒҜгҒқгҒ®гӮігӮ№гғҲгҒӘгҒ—гҖӮ (2) **гӮігғјгғүгӮөгӮӨгӮә**: libc гҒ® format и§ЈжһҗйғЁеҲҶгӮ’гғӘгғігӮҜгҒӣгҒҡгҖҒ гғҗгӮӨгғҠгғӘгӮ’е°ҸгҒ•гҒҸ (~1KB)гҖӮ (3) **е®үе…ЁжҖ§**: гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғјеҲ¶еҫЎгҒ® format ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮ’жҢҒгҒӨ printf гҒҜ **format-string vulnerability** гҒ®еҸӨе…ё (CVE гҒ®е®ҡз•Ә)вҖ”вҖ”fputs гҒ«гҒҜ format жҰӮеҝөгҒҢгҒӘгҒ„гҒ®гҒ§гҖҒ гғҰгғјгӮ¶гғје…ҘеҠӣгҒҢгғҮгғјгӮҝеҪ№еүІгҒӢгӮүйҖғгҒ’гӮүгӮҢгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ (4) **жҳҺзўәжҖ§**: гҖҢгҒ“гҒ®ж–Үеӯ—еҲ—гӮ’еҮәеҠӣгҖҚ гҒҜ `fputs(s, fd)` гҒ®ж–№гҒҢ `printf("%s", s)` гӮҲгӮҠзӣҙжҺҘзҡ„гҖӮ зҸҫд»ЈгҒ® GNU echo гҒҜ coreutils гҒ§ printf гҒЁ code гӮ’е…ұжңүгҒҷгӮӢеүҜж¬ЎеҠ№жһңгҒҜгҒӮгӮӢгҒҢгҖҒ 1979 е№ҙгҒ®иЁӯиЁҲгҒҜеҲҶйӣўгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒҹгҖӮ еҗҢгҒҳгҖҢfputs-over-printfгҖҚ еҺҹеүҮгҒҜзҸҫд»ЈгҒ® critical-path loggingгҖҒ embeddedгҖҒ kernel printk variant гҒ§гӮӮйҒ©з”ЁгҖӮ 1979 е№ҙгҒ® echo гҒҢгҖҒ гӮ»гӮӯгғҘгғӘгғҶгӮЈз ”з©¶иҖ…гҒҢ format-string и„ҶејұжҖ§гӮ’еҪўејҸеҢ–гҒҷгӮӢж•°еҚҒе№ҙеүҚгҒ«еҒ¶з„¶ гҖҢжӯЈгҒ—гҒ„йҒ“е…·гӮ’дҪҝгҒҶгҖҚ жҷәж…§гӮ’е®ҹи·өгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гҒҹгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: find uses 4-function recursive-descent parser (not yacc) for the predicate expression language. Functions handle precedence levels: exp() = -o, e1() = -a (explicit or implicit), e2() = !, e3() = parens + predicates. Implicit -a inserted when consecutive predicates lack operator. nxtarg() advances Ai index through argv, the input source вҖ” no separate lexer needed since argv pre-tokenized by shell. Randlast tracks 'last token was operand' to catch 'operand follows operand' errors. The hand-written parser is small (~80 lines) for a language with 3 operators + parens + ~11 predicates. Predates yacc availability or just deemed unnecessary for this size of grammar. Pattern: argv-as-input + recursive-descent parser appears in many small Unix tools (test, expr).
ai_analysis.human_story: yacc existed but find didn't use it вҖ” for a small predicate language, hand-written recursive descent is shorter and clearer. Four functions exp/e1/e2/e3 stack like nested boxes, each handling one precedence level. Implicit AND: write `find -name foo -mtime 7` and find auto-inserts AND between them вҖ” the parser detects 'two predicates in a row' and treats it as `-a`. This 'consecutive predicates AND together' is what makes find readable: you list conditions, not boolean operators. Half the find queries you'll ever write rely on implicit AND. 80 lines of hand parser give a domain-specific language with shell-script ergonomics.

appraiser_note: find гҒҜ **yacc гӮ’дҪҝгӮҸгҒҡ**гҖҒ 4 й–ўж•°гҒ® **еҶҚеё°дёӢйҷҚгғ‘гғјгӮө**гҒ§иҝ°иӘһејҸиЁҖиӘһгӮ’и§ЈжһҗгҖӮ й–ўж•°гҒҢе„Әе…Ҳй ҶдҪҚгғ¬гғҷгғ«гӮ’жӢ…еҪ“: exp() = -oгҖҒ e1() = -a (жҳҺзӨә/жҡ—й»ҷ)гҖҒ e2() = !гҖҒ e3() = жӢ¬еј§ + иҝ°иӘһгҖӮ йҖЈз¶ҡиҝ°иӘһгҒ«жј”з®—еӯҗз„ЎгҒ‘гӮҢгҒ° **жҡ—й»ҷгҒ® -a** гӮ’жҢҝе…ҘгҖӮ nxtarg() гҒҜ Ai гӮӨгғігғҮгғғгӮҜгӮ№гӮ’ argv гҒ«йҖІгӮҒгӮӢвҖ”вҖ”shell гҒҢдәӢеүҚгғҲгғјгӮҜгғіеҢ–жёҲгҒҝгҒӘгҒ®гҒ§еҲҘ lexer дёҚиҰҒгҖӮ Randlast гҒ§гҖҢзӣҙеүҚгҒҢ operand гҒӢгҖҚ гӮ’иҝҪи·ЎгҒ—гҖҒ гҖҢoperand follows operandгҖҚ гӮЁгғ©гғјгӮ’гӮӯгғЈгғғгғҒгҖӮ жүӢжӣёгҒҚгғ‘гғјгӮөгҒҜзҙ„ 80 иЎҢвҖ”вҖ”3 жј”з®—еӯҗ + жӢ¬еј§ + 11 иҝ°иӘһгҒ®ж–Үжі•гҒ«гҒҜеҚҒеҲҶгҖӮ yacc еҲ©з”ЁеҸҜиғҪгҒ гҒЈгҒҹгҒҢе°ҸиҰҸжЁЎж–Үжі•гҒ«гҒҜдёҚиҰҒгҒЁеҲӨж–ӯгҖӮ argv-as-input + еҶҚеё°дёӢйҷҚгғ‘гғјгӮөгҒ®гғ‘гӮҝгғјгғігҒҜеӨҡгҒҸгҒ®е°ҸгҒ•гҒӘ Unix гғ„гғјгғ« (testгҖҒ expr) гҒ§еҶҚеҲ©з”ЁгҖӮ йҖЈз¶ҡиҝ°иӘһгҒ®жҡ—й»ҷ AND гҒҢ find гӮ’иӘӯгҒҝгӮ„гҒҷгҒҸгҒҷгӮӢвҖ”вҖ”жқЎд»¶гӮ’еҲ—жҢҷгҒҷгӮӢгҒ гҒ‘гҒ§жј”з®—еӯҗгӮ’жӣёгҒӢгҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: descend() does depth-first recursive directory traversal. For each entry: stat to get metadata, evaluate predicate AST against current file (via *exlist->F(exlist)), if directory then chdir into it and recurse. Skip . and .. to avoid infinite recursion. V7 struct direct is 16 bytes (2-byte ino_t + 14-byte name); 512-byte block holds 32 entries (dsize >> 4). chdir-based traversal makes stat calls faster (shorter paths to inode lookup) at the cost of needing to remember Home to restore for -exec. Modern find uses fchdir(2) for thread/concurrency safety. Recursion depth = directory depth; deep trees can stack-overflow (no iterative alternative in 1979). The algorithm is preorder DFS: evaluate predicate before descending.
ai_analysis.human_story: Walking a directory tree: stat the entry, run predicates, if directory then descend. Skip . and .. (would loop forever). Read directory entries in 512-byte chunks (V7 directory format = array of 16-byte entries). 1979's chdir-based design: change to current directory, do work, recurse. Faster for stat (shorter paths) but loses absolute-position context вҖ” find remembers Home from popen('pwd') at startup. Modern find uses fchdir(2) for safety. The preorder DFS pattern вҖ” visit yourself, then visit children вҖ” is the canonical 'walk a tree' algorithm, applied here to filesystems.

appraiser_note: descend() гҒҜ **ж·ұгҒ•е„Әе…ҲеҶҚеё°**гҒ§гғҮгӮЈгғ¬гӮҜгғҲгғӘгғ„гғӘгғјгӮ’иө°жҹ»гҖӮ еҗ„гӮЁгғігғҲгғӘгҒ§: stat гҒ§гғЎгӮҝгғҮгғјгӮҝеҸ–еҫ—гҖҒ иҝ°иӘһ AST гӮ’и©•дҫЎ (`(*exlist->F)(exlist)`)гҖҒ гғҮгӮЈгғ¬гӮҜгғҲгғӘгҒӘгӮү chdir гҒ§е…ҘгҒЈгҒҰеҶҚеё°гҖӮ **. гҒЁ .. гӮ’гӮ№гӮӯгғғгғ—**гҒ—гҒҰз„ЎйҷҗеҶҚеё°еӣһйҒҝгҖӮ V7 struct direct гҒҜ 16 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ (2 гғҗгӮӨгғҲ ino_t + 14 гғҗгӮӨгғҲеҗҚ)гҖҒ 512 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜгҒ§ 32 гӮЁгғігғҲгғӘ (dsize >> 4)гҖӮ chdir гғҷгғјгӮ№иө°жҹ»гҒ§ stat гҒҢй«ҳйҖҹ (гғ‘гӮ№гҒҢзҹӯгҒҸ inode lookup гҒҢзҹӯгҒ„) гҒҢгҖҒ зө¶еҜҫдҪҚзҪ®жғ…е ұгӮ’еӨұгҒҶгҒ®гҒ§ -exec гҒ®гҒҹгӮҒ Home гӮ’ popen('pwd') гҒ§иө·еӢ•жҷӮиЁҳжҶ¶гҖӮ зҸҫд»Ј find гҒҜ fchdir(2) гҒ§гӮ№гғ¬гғғгғү/дёҰиЎҢе®үе…ЁгҖӮ еҶҚеё°ж·ұгҒ• = гғҮгӮЈгғ¬гӮҜгғҲгғӘж·ұгҒ•гҖҒ ж·ұгҒ„гғ„гғӘгғјгҒ§гӮ№гӮҝгғғгӮҜгӮӘгғјгғҗгғјгғ•гғӯгғјгҒ®еҸҜиғҪжҖ§ (1979 е№ҙгҒ« iterative д»ЈжӣҝгҒӘгҒ—)гҖӮ **preorder DFS** (иҮӘеҲҶгӮ’иЁӘе•ҸгҒ—гҒҰгҒӢгӮүеӯҗгҒё) гҒ®жЁҷжә–гӮўгғ«гӮҙгғӘгӮәгғ гӮ’гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«гӮ·гӮ№гғҶгғ гҒ«йҒ©з”ЁгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: -exec command {} \; is the most powerful find predicate. doex() builds nargv[] by walking from the saved arg position: copy each arg literally except substitute {} вҶ’ Pathname. Stop at ; sentinel. Fork: parent waits for child exit code; child chdir to original Home (so command runs with sensible cwd) and execvp. The \; is shell-escaped вҖ” bare ; would be a shell statement separator. -ok is interactive variant: print prompt, getchar() y/n, run only if 'y'. Both use static char *nargv[50] (max 50 args) reused across calls. Modern GNU find adds + variant: `-exec rm {} +` batches multiple files into one command (xargs-like). 1979 design is one-fork-per-file, simple and unambiguous.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you delete every .tmp file in /tmp? `find /tmp -name '*.tmp' -exec rm {} \;`. The {} is substitution: each matching file gets pasted into the command. The \; is the end marker, escaped from shell. Internally: fork, child changes back to original directory, execvp runs the command, parent waits. Repeat per matching file. -ok is the safe sibling: ask 'y/n?' before each execution. The 1979 design lets any Unix command be plugged into the find pipeline вҖ” perfect composability. Modern GNU find -exec ... + batches calls for efficiency. The \; vs + distinction is part of every find tutorial today.

appraiser_note: **-exec command {} \;** гҒҢ find гҒ®жңҖеј·иҝ°иӘһгҖӮ doex() гҒҢ nargv[] гӮ’ж§ӢзҜү: дҝқеӯҳгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹ arg дҪҚзҪ®гҒӢгӮүеҗ„еј•ж•°гӮ’гӮігғ”гғјгҖҒ {} гҒҜ Pathname гҒ§зҪ®жҸӣгҖҒ ; гӮ»гғігғҒгғҚгғ«гҒ§еҒңжӯўгҖӮ fork: иҰӘгҒҜеӯҗгҒ® exit code гӮ’ waitгҖҒ еӯҗгҒҜе…ғгҒ® Home гҒ« chdir гҒ—гҒҰгҒӢгӮү execvp (гӮігғһгғігғүгҒҢеҰҘеҪ“гҒӘ cwd гҒ§е®ҹиЎҢгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгӮҲгҒҶгҒ«)гҖӮ \; гҒҜ shell гҒӢгӮүе®ҲгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгӮЁгӮ№гӮұгғјгғ—вҖ”вҖ”иЈёгҒ® ; гҒҜ shell гҒ®ж–ҮеҢәеҲҮгӮҠгҖӮ **-ok гҒҜеҜҫи©ұзүҲ**: гғ—гғӯгғігғ—гғҲеҮәеҠӣгҖҒ getchar() гҒ§ y/nгҖҒ 'y' гҒӘгӮүе®ҹиЎҢгҖӮ дёЎж–№гҒЁгӮӮ static char *nargv[50] (жңҖеӨ§ 50 еј•ж•°) гӮ’е‘јгҒіеҮәгҒ—й–“гҒ§еҶҚеҲ©з”ЁгҖӮ зҸҫд»Ј GNU find гҒҜ **+** гғҗгғӘгӮўгғігғҲ (`-exec rm {} +`) гҒ§иӨҮж•°гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«гӮ’ 1 гӮігғһгғігғүгҒ«гғҗгғғгғҒеҢ– (xargs гғ©гӮӨгӮҜ)гҖӮ 1979 иЁӯиЁҲгҒҜ 1 гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ« 1 forkвҖ”вҖ”еҚҳзҙ”гҒ§жӣ–жҳ§гҒ•гҒӘгҒ—гҖӮ `\;` vs `+` гҒ®еҢәеҲҘгҒҜзҸҫд»Ј find гғҒгғҘгғјгғҲгғӘгӮўгғ«гҒ®е®ҡз•ӘгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: find's AST nodes are struct anode with 3 fields: F (function pointer to predicate), L/R (left/right children). Node[100] static array, mk() allocates, Nn counter. Evaluation: call (*node->F)(node) вҖ” predicate functions are passed the node itself, can access L/R for binary ops, can cast for predicate-specific data layout. The cast is implicit in K&R C: mtime() declares its arg as `struct { int f, t, s; } *p` вҖ” reinterprets anode storage as predicate-specific layout. Modern C compilers warn about this; K&R C accepted it. The pattern is interpreter pattern (Gang of Four design pattern, published 1994) implemented in 1979. Same architecture: predicates = leaf nodes, and/or/not = internal nodes, evaluation = tree walk.
ai_analysis.human_story: find's predicates are first-class values: each is a function pointer. The AST is built from anode structs (function + L + R). Evaluating = calling the function with the node as argument; and/or/not call their children's functions, others do the actual work. Predicates reinterpret the anode storage to access their own arg layout вҖ” a trick that works because K&R C didn't type-check function pointers. The pattern (Interpreter Pattern in Gang of Four 1994 terminology) implemented in 1979's C. Same architecture as every modern tree-walking interpreter: leaf = primitive, internal = combinator. find is its early Unix example.

appraiser_note: find гҒ® AST гғҺгғјгғүгҒҜ struct anodeгҖҒ 3 гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғү: F (иҝ°иӘһй–ўж•°гғқгӮӨгғігӮҝ)гҖҒ L/R (е·ҰеҸігҒ®еӯҗ)гҖӮ Node[100] йқҷзҡ„й…ҚеҲ—гҖҒ mk() гҒ§еүІеҪ“гҖҒ Nn гӮ«гӮҰгғігӮҝгҖӮ и©•дҫЎ: `(*node->F)(node)` вҖ”вҖ”иҝ°иӘһй–ўж•°гҒ«гғҺгғјгғүиҮӘиә«гӮ’жёЎгҒ—гҖҒ дәҢй …жј”з®—гҒ§гҒҜ L/R гҒ«гӮўгӮҜгӮ»гӮ№гҖҒ иҝ°иӘһеӣәжңүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғ¬гӮӨгӮўгӮҰгғҲгҒ§ cast еҸҜиғҪгҖӮ K&R C гҒ§гҒҜгӮӯгғЈгӮ№гғҲгҒҢжҡ—й»ҷ: mtime() гҒҜеј•ж•°гӮ’ `struct { int f, t, s; } *p` гҒЁе®ЈиЁҖвҖ”вҖ”anode гҒ® storage гӮ’иҝ°иӘһеӣәжңүгғ¬гӮӨгӮўгӮҰгғҲгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰеҶҚи§ЈйҮҲгҖӮ зҸҫд»Ј C гӮігғігғ‘гӮӨгғ©гҒҜ warning гҒҷгӮӢгҒҢ K&R C гҒҜйҖҡгҒҷгҖӮ гҒ“гӮҢгҒҜ **Interpreter Pattern** (Gang of Four 1994 гҒ®гғҮгӮ¶гӮӨгғігғ‘гӮҝгғјгғі) гӮ’ 1979 е№ҙгҒ® C гҒ§е®ҹиЈ…гҖӮ еҗҢгҒҳгӮўгғјгӮӯгғҶгӮҜгғҒгғЈ: иҝ°иӘһ = leafгҖҒ and/or/not = internalгҖҒ и©•дҫЎ = tree walkгҖӮ гҒҷгҒ№гҒҰгҒ®зҸҫд»Ј tree-walking interpreter (Lisp evalгҖҒ Python AST и©•дҫЎгҖҒ SQL executor) гҒ®ж—©жңҹ Unix дҫӢгҖӮ

ai_analysis.technical_context: find exposes struct stat fields as predicates: mtime/atime/ctime (timestamps in days via /A_DAY=86400), user/group (uid/gid lookup via getunum from /etc/passwd or /etc/group), size (in 512-byte blocks via (size+511)>>9 = ceiling division), perm (mode & 07777, with '-' meaning at-least-these-bits), links (st_nlink), type (st_mode & S_IFMT). Shared scomp() handles +N (greater), -N (less), N (equal) for all numeric predicates. Modern GNU find adds size suffixes (c=bytes, k=KB, M=MB, G=GB), -size with /N for exact, more time predicates (mtime/atime/ctime + -min variants), -regex (vs -name's glob), -printf for formatted output. The 1979 set is the foundation: 11 predicates covering all key stat fields.
ai_analysis.human_story: Want files modified in the last week? `-mtime -7`. Owned by alice? `-user alice`. Larger than 100KB? `-size +200` (200 blocks = 100KB). Permissions 0755? `-perm 755`. find's 11 predicates expose struct stat as a query language. scomp() unifies '+N greater', '-N less', 'N equal' across all numeric predicates вҖ” write the comparison once. -perm with '-' prefix means 'at least these bits' (set bits required, others may be set too) вҖ” a common gotcha. 1979's set covers all the basics; modern GNU find layers convenience features on top, but the foundation is here.

appraiser_note: find гҒҜ **struct stat гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғүгӮ’иҝ°иӘһгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰе…¬й–Ӣ**: mtime/atime/ctime (гӮҝгӮӨгғ гӮ№гӮҝгғігғ—гҖҒ /A_DAY=86400 гҒ§ж—ҘеҚҳдҪҚ)гҖҒ user/group (uid/gid lookup via getunum гҒ§ /etc/passwd гӮ„ /etc/group жӨңзҙў)гҖҒ size (512 гғҗгӮӨгғҲгғ–гғӯгғғгӮҜгҖҒ (size+511)>>9 = ceiling йҷӨз®—)гҖҒ perm (mode & 07777гҖҒ '-' гҒҜ at-least-these-bits)гҖҒ links (st_nlink)гҖҒ type (st_mode & S_IFMT)гҖӮ **е…ұйҖҡ scomp()** гҒҢ +N (гӮҲгӮҠеӨ§гҒҚгҒ„)гҖҒ -N (гӮҲгӮҠе°ҸгҒ•гҒ„)гҖҒ N (зӯүгҒ—гҒ„) гӮ’е…Ёж•°еҖӨиҝ°иӘһгҒ§жүұгҒҶвҖ”вҖ”жҜ”ијғгғӯгӮёгғғгӮҜгӮ’ 1 з®ҮжүҖгҒ§иЁҳиҝ°гҖӮ зҸҫд»Ј GNU find гҒҜгӮөгӮӨгӮәгӮөгғ•гӮЈгғғгӮҜгӮ№ (c=гғҗгӮӨгғҲгҖҒ k=KBгҖҒ M=MBгҖҒ G=GB)гҖҒ -size гҒ« /N гҒ§е®Ңе…ЁдёҖиҮҙгҖҒ гӮҲгӮҠеӨҡгҒҸгҒ®жҷӮеҲ»иҝ°иӘһ (-min гғҗгғӘгӮўгғігғҲ)гҖҒ -regex (vs -name гҒ® glob)гҖҒ -printf еҮәеҠӣгғ•гӮ©гғјгғһгғғгғҲгӮ’иҝҪеҠ гҖӮ 1979 е№ҙгҒ®гӮ»гғғгғҲгҒҜеҹәзӣӨвҖ”вҖ”11 иҝ°иӘһгҒ§дё»иҰҒ stat гғ•гӮЈгғјгғ«гғүгӮ’е…ЁйғЁгӮ«гғҗгғјгҖӮ SQL гҒ® WHERE owner='alice' AND modified_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days' гҒЁжҰӮеҝөзҡ„гҒ«еҗҢгҒҳ DSL гӮ’ 1979 е№ҙгҒ«гҖӮ
